title: emperor
description: First emperor of unified China. Conquered the six rival Warring States (Han, Zhao, Wei, Chu, Yan, Qi) between 230-221 BCE and proclaimed himself Shi Huangdi ('First August Emperor'). Standardized script, weights, measures, axle widths; built the first unified Great Wall defensive line; completed the massive Lingqu canal; commissioned the terracotta army mausoleum at Lishan.
notable_events: -221 unification of China, adoption of title Huangdi
-213 burning of books (fenshu) ordered by chancellor Li Si
-212 burying alive of ~460 scholars (kengru) at Xianyang
-210 death during inspection tour, succession crisis

sources.citation: Sima Qian. Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian), c. 94 BCE, Basic Annals of Qin Shi Huang.
Bodde, Derk. China's First Unifier: A Study of the Ch'in Dynasty as Seen in the Life of Li Ssu. Leiden: Brill, 1938 (repr. 1967).
Lewis, Mark Edward. The Early Chinese Empires: Qin and Han. Harvard University Press, 2007.

name_variants.name: Ying Zheng
name_variants.context: birth name (嬴政), before imperial title

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Qin Shi Huangdi's administrative achievements came at a catastrophic human cost. The burning of books (213 BCE) targeted the Classics and histories of the conquered states, an act of deliberate cultural erasure. The kengru (often mistranslated as 'burying of Confucians') executed ~460 scholars accused of circulating forbidden texts. Construction of the Great Wall, Lingqu canal, Epang palace and the Lishan mausoleum used hundreds of thousands of conscripted laborers and convicts; death tolls on these projects, while not precisely quantifiable, are described in Sima Qian as enormous. Legalist penal code applied collective punishment (familial execution to three degrees). Pre-modern Chinese historiography (especially Jia Yi's Guo Qin Lun) judged the dynasty harshly; 20th-c. Mao-era rehabilitation of Qin Shi Huang was itself ideologically motivated.

title: emperor
description: Seventh emperor of the Western Han. Expanded Han territory decisively: campaigns against the Xiongnu (under Wei Qing and Huo Qubing) pushed the steppe frontier north; the Hexi Corridor and Tarim Basin oases were incorporated, opening the Silk Road; Nanyue (modern Guangdong/northern Vietnam) was annexed in 111 BCE; Gojoseon fell in 108 BCE, creating the Four Commanderies in the Korean peninsula. Established Confucianism as state orthodoxy via Dong Zhongshu's reforms and the Imperial Academy (太學, 124 BCE).
notable_events: -138 first mission of Zhang Qian to the Western Regions
-119 Battle of Mobei, decisive defeat of Xiongnu

sources.citation: Sima Qian. Shiji, Annals of Emperor Wu (c. 94 BCE).
Ban Gu. Hanshu (Book of Han), Annals of Emperor Wu, c. 111 CE.
Loewe, Michael. A Biographical Dictionary of the Qin, Former Han and Xin Periods. Leiden: Brill, 2000.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Wu's expansion, particularly the Xiongnu wars, mobilized the Han state to exhaustion; Sima Qian — himself castrated under Wu for defending general Li Ling — documented the fiscal crisis, the sale of ranks and convict labor, and the ruinous taxation. The 108 BCE conquest of Gojoseon destroyed an independent Korean polity; the Four Commanderies were a colonial apparatus. The 'witch trials' of 91 BCE (wugu zhi huo) killed thousands including the crown prince and empress. His sumptuary impulse toward immortality cults is treated in the Shiji with implicit criticism.

title: emperor
description: Second Tang emperor. Came to power in 626 through the Xuanwu Gate Incident (玄武門之變) — he personally killed his elder brother Crown Prince Li Jiancheng and younger brother Li Yuanji, then forced his father Li Yuan to abdicate. His Zhenguan reign (貞觀) became the Confucian paradigm of good government, codified in the Zhenguan Zhengyao. Subdued the Eastern Turkic Khaganate (630), recognized as Tengri Qaghan by steppe peoples, opened the Silk Road, patronized Xuanzang's pilgrimage to India.
notable_events: 626 Xuanwu Gate Incident, fratricide and coup
630 defeat of Eastern Turkic Khaganate, assumes title 'Heavenly Khagan'
641 Princess Wencheng married to Tibetan Tsenpo Songtsen Gampo

sources.citation: Wu Jing. Zhenguan Zhengyao (Essentials of Government from the Zhenguan Period), 8th c.
Liu Xu. Jiu Tangshu (Old Book of Tang), 945 CE.
Wechsler, Howard J. 'T'ai-tsung (reign 626-49) the Consolidator,' in Twitchett (ed.), Cambridge History of China vol. 3, Cambridge University Press, 1979.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Zhenguan-era propaganda — which Taizong personally supervised by reviewing the draft veritable records — systematically whitewashed the 626 fratricide and the forced retirement of his father. Wechsler (1979) and Bingham (1941) both demonstrate the Tang founding was more brutal than official narrative admitted. The 641 Wencheng marriage, while often romanticized, was part of a tribute-and-hostage diplomatic apparatus that later crumbled (Tang-Tibet wars 670-821).

title: empress regnant
description: Only woman in Chinese imperial history to rule in her own name with the title huangdi. Concubine of Taizong, later consort of Gaozong; de facto regent after Gaozong's stroke in 660; after his death in 683 deposed her sons Zhongzong and Ruizong; in 690 proclaimed her own Zhou (周) dynasty, suspending the Tang for 15 years. Restored Tang by the Shenlong coup of 705, weeks before her death.
notable_events: 655 elevated to empress consort after deposition of Wang
660 effective regency begins after Gaozong's stroke
690 proclamation of Zhou dynasty, own reign as emperor
705 Shenlong coup, forced abdication, restoration of Tang

sources.citation: Sima Guang. Zizhi Tongjian, 1084 CE (juan 199-208).
Ouyang Xiu. Xin Tangshu (New Book of Tang), 1060 CE.
Guisso, Richard W. L. Wu Tse-T'ien and the Politics of Legitimation in T'ang China. Bellingham: Western Washington University, 1978.
Rothschild, N. Harry. Wu Zhao: China's Only Woman Emperor. Pearson Longman, 2008.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002/007: Traditional Confucian historiography (Sima Guang, Ouyang Xiu) demonized Wu as a usurper who poisoned relatives and employed cruel ministers like Lai Junchen; modern scholarship (Guisso 1978, Rothschild 2008) treats these accounts as compromised by anti-female bias, but documents real purges of Tang princes and Li-clan descendants during her consolidation. She did institutionalize a climate of political terror 684-697. Simultaneously she opened the civil-service examination to broader participation, patronized Chan Buddhism, and appointed several capable officials (Di Renjie, Lou Shide). Both truths must be kept.

name_regnal: 啟運立極英武睿文神德聖功至明大孝皇帝
title: emperor
description: Founder of the Song dynasty. General of the Later Zhou, seized the throne in the Chenqiao Mutiny (陳橋兵變, 960) when his troops draped the imperial yellow robe on him. Over the next 16 years reunified most of China south of the Wall, ending the Five Dynasties period. Famous for 'cup of wine' (杯酒釋兵權, 961) in which he peacefully persuaded senior generals to retire, decisively subordinating the military to civilian bureaucracy for three centuries.
notable_events: 961 'Cup of wine releasing military power' banquet
963-975 conquests of Jingnan, Hunan, Later Shu, Southern Han, Southern Tang

sources.citation: Li Tao. Xu Zizhi Tongjian Changbian, 12th c.
Toqto'a et al. Songshi, 1345 CE.
Lau, Nap-yin and Huang, K'uan-chung. 'Founding and Consolidation of the Sung Dynasty,' in Cambridge History of China vol. 5, 2009.

ethical_notes: The circumstances of the 976 succession (the 'Axe Sound and Candle Shadow' 燭影斧聲) — in which Taizu died suddenly after a night drinking with his brother Taizong, who then succeeded rather than Taizu's sons — is one of Chinese history's most enduring unresolved questions. Contemporary sources under Taizong were censored; the later tradition that Taizong murdered him is circumstantial but cannot be disproven.

title: khagan/emperor
description: Grandson of Chinggis Khan. Proclaimed Great Khan at Shangdu in 1260, winning a four-year civil war (Toluid Civil War) against his brother Ariq Boke; proclaimed the Yuan dynasty in 1271, completing the conquest of Southern Song in 1279 at the Battle of Yamen, where chief minister Lu Xiufu leapt into the sea with the nine-year-old emperor Bing. Two failed invasions of Japan (1274 Bun'ei and 1281 Koan) and a failed invasion of Java (1293). Patron of Tibetan Buddhism; employed Marco Polo and Phags-pa Lama.
notable_events: 1260 proclamation as Great Khan, break with the western uluses
1274, 1281 failed invasions of Japan, storms (kamikaze) destroy fleets
1279 Battle of Yamen, end of Southern Song

sources.citation: Song Lian et al. Yuanshi, 1370 CE.
Rashid al-Din. Jami al-tawarikh, c. 1310 CE.
Rossabi, Morris. Khubilai Khan: His Life and Times. University of California Press, 1988.

name_variants.name: Qubilai Qa'an
name_variants.context: titular form used in Mongol court

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Yuan conquest of the Southern Song (1268-1279) killed millions through siege, famine, and the sack of cities like Changzhou (1275, where Bayan reportedly ordered a mass killing). The population of China contracted sharply between the Jin censuses of ~1207 (~55 million households) and the Yuan census of 1290 (~14 million households); while classification differences account for part of the gap, the net loss from Mongol conquest in northern China alone is estimated by modern demographers (Ho Ping-ti, Wu Songdi) in the tens of millions. Yuan social hierarchy (Mongol > Semu > Han > Nanren) was a formalized ethnic caste system.

title: emperor
description: Founder of the Ming dynasty. Born to a destitute peasant family in Haozhou (Anhui); lost parents to famine and plague at 16; took refuge as a Buddhist novice. Joined the Red Turban rebellion in 1352, rose rapidly, took Nanjing in 1356, defeated rival Chen Youliang at Lake Poyang (1363), and proclaimed the Ming in Nanjing in 1368. Expelled the Yuan court northward. Established the rigid Six Ministries system, the hereditary military guard (weisuo) system, the huangce population registers, and the fishscale land registers.
notable_events: 1363 Battle of Lake Poyang, defeat of Chen Youliang
1368 proclamation of Ming, capture of Dadu
1380 Hu Weiyong case, abolition of the chancellorship
1393 Lan Yu case, decimation of the military elite

sources.citation: Ming Shilu, Taizu Shilu (official veritable records, 1418 revision).
Zhang Tingyu et al. Mingshi, 1739 CE.
Mote, Frederick W. Imperial China 900-1800. Harvard University Press, 1999.
Dardess, John W. Ming China, 1368-1644: A Concise History of a Resilient Empire. Rowman & Littlefield, 2012.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Hongwu's reign was marked by sustained political terror. The Hu Weiyong case (1380) and Lan Yu case (1393) each executed ~15,000-30,000 officials, generals, and their families by familial extermination (誅族). The Kongyin Case and Guo Huan case each killed tens of thousands. Mingshi estimates ~100,000 executed across his 30-year reign, though scholars (Dardess) consider the number a lower bound. He codified punishments including slicing (lingchi) and re-introduced the practice of royal concubines being forced to commit suicide at the emperor's death (immolation/strangulation) — abolished only in 1464.

title: emperor
description: Third Ming emperor. Fourth son of Hongwu; seized the throne from his nephew Jianwen in the Jingnan Campaign civil war (1399-1402). Moved the capital to Beijing (1421), built the Forbidden City, commissioned the Yongle Encyclopedia (永樂大典, the largest encyclopedia produced anywhere before the 18th c.), sponsored Zheng He's seven treasure voyages (1405-1433) that reached East Africa, and personally led five northern campaigns against the Mongols.
notable_events: 1399-1402 Jingnan Campaign, civil war against Jianwen

sources.citation: Ming Shilu, Taizong Shilu.
Dreyer, Edward L. Zheng He: China and the Oceans in the Early Ming Dynasty. Pearson Longman, 2007.
Chan, Hok-lam. 'The Chien-wen, Yung-lo, Hung-hsi and Hsuan-te reigns,' in Cambridge History of China vol. 7, 1988.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: To erase the Jianwen-restoration faction Yongle ordered mass familial extermination (the 'Ten Clans' punishment against Fang Xiaoru reportedly killed 873 people). The Dai Viet occupation (1407-1427) involved systematic destruction of Vietnamese texts and imposition of Chinese administration — an act Vietnamese historiography treats as a cultural genocide that triggered the Lam Son uprising under Le Loi. The treasure voyages, while often romanticized, were explicit demonstrations of naval force: the fleet intervened in Sri Lankan politics (1411 abduction of King Alakeshvara), suppressed piracy in the Straits, and demanded tribute.

title: emperor
description: Fourth Qing emperor. Took the throne at age 7; ruled in his own name from 1669 after arresting regent Oboi. Suppressed the Revolt of the Three Feudatories (1673-1681). Annexed Taiwan (1683) ending the Ming loyalist Zheng regime. Defeated the Dzungars at the Battle of Jao Modo (1696). Signed the Treaty of Nerchinsk with Russia (1689) — the first equal-parties treaty China signed with a European power. Patron of Jesuit astronomers and of the Kangxi Dictionary (康熙字典, 1716).
notable_events: 1696 Battle of Jao Modo, rout of Galdan Khan

sources.citation: Qing Shilu, Shengzu Shilu.
Spence, Jonathan. Emperor of China: Self-Portrait of K'ang-hsi. New York: Knopf, 1974.
Perdue, Peter C. China Marches West: The Qing Conquest of Central Eurasia. Harvard University Press, 2005.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Revolt suppression (1673-1681) involved massacres in Sichuan, Yunnan, and Fujian on a scale sufficient to depopulate Sichuan, requiring later state-sponsored repopulation. The Dzungar campaigns, which Kangxi launched and Qianlong completed, ultimately produced a genocide documented by Perdue (2005) — roughly 80% of the Dzungar population killed or displaced by 1757-58. The Kangxi coastal evacuation (遷海令, renewed 1661-1683) against Ming loyalists forcibly displaced populations 10-30 li inland along the Fujian-Guangdong coast, causing mass death from starvation and exposure.

title: emperor
description: Sixth Qing emperor. Formally abdicated in 1796 so as not to exceed his grandfather Kangxi's 61-year reign, but retained real power until his death in 1799. Under his reign the Qing reached its maximum territorial extent: the Ten Great Campaigns (十全武功) included the final conquest of the Dzungar Khanate (1755-1757), the Altishahr/Xinjiang conquest (1759), and costly wars against Burma, Vietnam, and the Gurkhas. Commissioned the Siku Quanshu (四庫全書, 1773-1782), a library of 36,000+ volumes.
notable_events: 1755-1757 conquest and genocide of the Dzungar Khanate
1759 annexation of the Altishahr / establishment of Xinjiang

sources.citation: Qing Shilu, Gaozong Shilu.
Perdue, Peter C. China Marches West. 2005.
Elliott, Mark C. Emperor Qianlong: Son of Heaven, Man of the World. Longman, 2009.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Dzungar campaigns of 1755-1757 were explicitly genocidal. Qianlong's own edicts ordered the killing of all Dzungar men capable of bearing arms; Perdue (2005) and Millward (2007) concur that roughly 500,000-600,000 Dzungars — perhaps 80% of the population — died by massacre, smallpox, or flight. The Jinchuan campaigns (1747-1749, 1771-1776) in western Sichuan destroyed the Gyalrong Tibetan stone-tower culture. The literary inquisition (文字獄) under Qianlong executed or exiled hundreds of scholars for perceived Ming loyalism or slurs against Manchus; the Siku Quanshu compilation doubled as a census-and-burn of suppressed texts.

title: emperor
description: 122nd emperor of Japan in the traditional count. Figurehead of the Meiji Restoration (明治維新, 1868) which abolished the shogunate and daimyō, centralized power in the emperor, abolished the samurai stipend system (1876), built a conscript army, industrialized, and adopted a Prussian-model constitution (1889). Under his reign Japan defeated Qing China (1894-95) and Imperial Russia (1904-05), annexed Ryukyu (1879), Taiwan (1895), southern Sakhalin (1905), and Korea (1910).
notable_events: 1868 Meiji Restoration proclaimed, Tokyo as capital
1895 Treaty of Shimonoseki, annexation of Taiwan
1905 Treaty of Portsmouth, southern Sakhalin and Korean protectorate

sources.citation: Meiji Tenno Ki (official chronicle of Meiji's reign), compiled 1933.
Keene, Donald. Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852-1912. Columbia University Press, 2002.
Beasley, W. G. The Meiji Restoration. Stanford University Press, 1972.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Meiji's personal agency is contested (Keene 2002 treats him as more involved than the oligarchic 'genro' narrative suggests). Under his reign Japan pursued colonial violence: the Taiwan pacification (1895-1902) killed tens of thousands of Hakka and Indigenous resisters; the suppression of the 1919 Korean March First Movement (posthumous reign legacy) killed 7,500+. The 1879 annexation of the Ryukyu Kingdom abolished an independent polity. The Ainu assimilation policy (Hokkaido Former Aborigines Protection Act, 1899) enacted cultural genocide. Japanese imperial propaganda cultivated emperor-worship (tennōsei) that provided ideological cover for 20th-c. atrocities.

title: daimyō / hegemon
description: First of Japan's three 'unifiers' of the Sengoku period. Defeated Imagawa Yoshimoto at Okehazama (1560); seized Kyoto in 1568 installing Ashikaga Yoshiaki as puppet shogun, whom he later expelled in 1573 ending the Muromachi shogunate. Destroyed the Enryaku-ji temple complex on Mount Hiei (1571) massacring monks, women, and children. Killed by his retainer Akechi Mitsuhide at Honnō-ji in Kyoto (1582).
notable_events: 1568 entry into Kyoto, puppet shogun installed
1573 expulsion of Ashikaga Yoshiaki, end of Muromachi shogunate
1580 end of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji war

sources.citation: Ota Gyuichi. Shinchō-kō ki (Chronicle of Lord Nobunaga), c. 1610.
Luis Frois. Historia de Japam, c. 1597.
Lamers, Jeroen. Japonius Tyrannus: The Japanese Warlord Oda Nobunaga Reconsidered. Hotei Publishing, 2000.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Nobunaga's campaigns against ikkō-ikki peasant-Buddhist leagues and against the Tendai establishment were unusually brutal even by Sengoku standards. Enryaku-ji (1571): reportedly 3,000-4,000 killed including non-combatants and children; Frois corroborates Japanese sources. Nagashima sieges (1571, 1573, 1574) culminated in burning ~20,000 ikkō-ikki and their families alive in the final siege. The epithet 'Tyrannus' in Frois reflects European observer alarm. His violence nonetheless ended the Sengoku fragmentation.

title: shōgun
description: Third 'unifier' and founder of the Tokugawa shogunate. Ally then vassal-ally of Nobunaga and Hideyoshi; after Hideyoshi's death in 1598, won the decisive Battle of Sekigahara (1600), was appointed sei-i tai-shōgun in 1603, founded the bakuhan system that would govern Japan until 1868. Resigned formally in 1605 to his son Hidetada but retained power as ogosho until his death. Destroyed the Toyotomi rump in the Osaka sieges of 1614-1615, eliminating the last rival.
notable_events: 1614-1615 Osaka Winter and Summer campaigns, destruction of Toyotomi Hideyori

sources.citation: Tokugawa Jikki (Veritable Records of the Tokugawa), 1809-1849.
Totman, Conrad. Tokugawa Ieyasu: Shogun. Heian, 1983.
Sadler, A. L. The Maker of Modern Japan: The Life of Tokugawa Ieyasu. George Allen & Unwin, 1937.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The system Ieyasu founded persecuted Christians under Hidetada and Iemitsu, culminating in the 1637-38 Shimabara Rebellion suppression (~37,000 killed). The sankin-kōtai system concentrated wealth on Edo at the expense of rural populations. The burakumin/eta caste formalization dates to the early Tokugawa codes. The 1615 destruction of Osaka Castle and mass killing of Toyotomi loyalists was extreme even by early-modern standards.

title: king
description: Fourth king of Joseon, widely regarded as Korea's greatest monarch. Invented or supervised the invention of Hangul (한글, promulgated 1446 as Hunminjeongeum), a phonetic script designed so that 'a wise man can acquaint himself with it before the morning is over.' Patron of the Jiphyeonjeon (Hall of Worthies) which produced the rain gauge (cheugugi, 1441), Korean astronomical instruments, and the Nongsa Jikseol agricultural manual. Expanded Joseon's northern border to the Yalu and Tumen rivers by establishing the Four Forts and Six Garrisons (사군육진).
notable_events: 1441 invention of cheugugi (rain gauge), 200 years before Europe

sources.citation: Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Veritable Records of the Joseon Dynasty), Sejong Sillok.
Hunminjeongeum Haerye, 1446.
Lee, Peter H. (ed.). Sourcebook of Korean Civilization, Volume I. Columbia University Press, 1993.
Eckert, Carter J. et al. Korea Old and New: A History. Ilchokak/Harvard, 1990.

ethical_notes: The yangban-literati who opposed Hangul (e.g. Choe Malli's 1444 memorial) argued it would 'abandon China and become barbarians'; this documents an elite resistance to vernacular literacy that would delay Hangul's full adoption until the 19th century. Sejong's reign was not without coercion: the Six Garrisons displacement expelled Jurchen populations from the northern frontier, and the 1419 Tsushima raid killed ~700 Wakō pirates and non-combatants.

title: kampaku / regent
description: Second unifier of Japan. Peasant-born; rose through Oda Nobunaga's service, avenged him after Honnō-ji (1582), defeated rival Shibata Katsuie at Shizugatake (1583), took Kyushu (1587) and the Hōjō of Odawara (1590), completing unification. As he was not a Minamoto he could not become shōgun, so adopted the court title kampaku (regent). Launched two catastrophic invasions of Korea (Imjin War, 1592-1598 / 임진왜란) that devastated the Korean peninsula and ended in Japanese withdrawal on his death.
notable_events: 1590 siege of Odawara, completion of unification

sources.citation: Ota Gyuichi. Taikō-sama Gunki no Uchi.
Yu Seong-nyong. Jingbirok (The Book of Corrections), 1604.
Berry, Mary Elizabeth. Hideyoshi. Harvard University Press, 1982.
Turnbull, Stephen. Samurai Invasion: Japan's Korean War 1592-98. Cassell, 2002.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Imjin War (임진왜란) devastated Korea. Estimates range from 1-2 million Korean deaths from war, famine, and plague — ~15-20% of the population. Systematic destruction of cultural sites (Gyeongbokgung burned), abduction of 50,000-100,000 Korean potters and craftsmen, and the 'ear mounds' (耳塚 / 鼻塚, Mimizuka) in Kyoto containing ~38,000 noses taken as proof of kills — still extant. Hideyoshi's 1587 expulsion edict against Christians foreshadowed the Tokugawa persecution; the 1597 crucifixion of the 26 Martyrs of Nagasaki was ordered by him. His conquest was driven by personal megalomania as documented in letters promising the Ming throne to his nephew.

title: emperor
description: Founder of the Han dynasty. Minor local official (tingzhang) of peasant origins in Pei County; joined the anti-Qin insurrection in 209 BCE; entered the Qin capital Xianyang in 206 BCE accepting the surrender of Ziying. Defeated rival Xiang Yu in the four-year Chu-Han contention, winning the decisive Battle of Gaixia in 202 BCE. First commoner to found a Chinese imperial dynasty.
notable_events: -206 enters Xianyang, receives surrender of Qin
-202 Battle of Gaixia, victory over Xiang Yu
-200 defeat at Baideng by Modu Chanyu of Xiongnu, humiliating heqin settlement

sources.citation: Sima Qian. Shiji, Basic Annals of Emperor Gaozu.
Twitchett, D. and Loewe, M. (eds.). Cambridge History of China Vol. 1: The Ch'in and Han Empires. 1986.

ethical_notes: The Han founding narrative celebrated in Shiji was shaped under his descendants; the purge of the 'Three Meritorious' (三傑 killed or exiled) — Han Xin (executed 196 BCE by Empress Lü on apparent Liu Bang sanction), Peng Yue (executed 196 BCE), Ying Bu (killed 195 BCE) — suggests a pattern of systematic elimination of rival warlords. The Empress Lü's brutal treatment of Concubine Qi (made into 'human swine' 人彘) after Liu Bang's death is recorded by Sima Qian.

title: king
description: Founder of the Joseon dynasty which lasted until 1897. General of the late Goryeo kingdom; in 1388 ordered to invade the Ming-held Liaodong peninsula, he turned his army back from the Yalu at Wihwa Island (威化島回軍), seized Kaesong, and after four years of political maneuvering deposed the Goryeo king in 1392, founding Joseon. Adopted Neo-Confucianism as state orthodoxy and transferred the capital to Hanyang (Seoul) in 1394.
notable_events: 1398 First Strife of Princes, abdication in favor of son Jeongjong

sources.citation: Joseon Wangjo Sillok, Taejo Sillok.
Duncan, John B. The Origins of the Chosŏn Dynasty. University of Washington Press, 2000.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The 1388 coup was a military seizure of power; Duncan (2000) argues the 'Neo-Confucian revolution' framing obscures that Joseon's literati-yangban oligarchy rapidly concretized a rigid class system including hereditary slavery (nobi) which at its 17th-c. peak constituted ~30% of the population. The 1398 First Strife of Princes in which his son Yi Bang-won (later Taejong) killed rival princes and key officials was a continuation of the founding violence.

title: khagan
description: Founder of the Mongol Empire, the largest contiguous land empire in history. Proclaimed Chinggis Qa'an at the 1206 kurultai on the Onon river after decades of tribal warfare. Campaigns against the Western Xia (1209-1227), Jin dynasty (1211-1234, completed by successors), Khwarazm Shahdom (1219-1221 — one of history's most destructive invasions), and across Persia and Rus' via his generals Jebe and Subutai.
notable_events: 1219-1221 invasion of Khwarazm, destruction of Samarkand, Bukhara, Merv, Urgench

sources.citation: Secret History of the Mongols (Mongγol-un niγuca tobčiyan), c. 1240 CE.
Rashid al-Din. Jami al-tawarikh, c. 1310.
Ata-Malik Juvayni. Tarikh-i Jahangushay, c. 1260.
Ratchnevsky, Paul. Genghis Khan: His Life and Legacy. Blackwell, 1991.
May, Timothy. The Mongol Conquests in World History. Reaktion, 2012.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Mongol conquests constitute one of history's deadliest events. Persian sources (Juvayni, Ibn al-Athir) report figures of 1.3 million killed at Merv alone, 1.7 million at Nishapur, and 1.6 million at Herat; modern scholars (McEvedy, Jones) treat these as exaggerations but still estimate 10-40 million deaths across the Chinggis and immediate-successor campaigns, with Khwarazm's population falling ~75%. Mongol warfare systematically targeted civilians to terrorize neighbors into surrender. Juvayni, serving Mongol masters, wrote that in Merv 'the sword was drawn and the tongue thrust into the sheath' — an explicit description of genocide. Simultaneously the Pax Mongolica enabled the Silk Road revival, religious pluralism in the yarlighs, and Eurasian cultural exchange. Both truths are essential.

title: amir
description: Founder of the Timurid Empire centered on Samarkand. Turco-Mongol of the Barlas clan; never claimed the title khan (he was not a Chinggisid) but ruled through puppet Chaghatayid khans as sahib-qiran ('lord of the auspicious conjunction'). Campaigns devastated Persia, the Caucasus, northern India (sack of Delhi, 1398), the Levant (sack of Damascus, 1401), and Anatolia (capture of Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I at Ankara, 1402).
notable_events: 1370 proclamation at Balkh, marriage to Saray Mulk Khanum
1402 Battle of Ankara, capture of Bayezid I
1405 death at Otrar en route to China

sources.citation: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi. Zafarnama, c. 1425.
Ibn Arabshah. Ajaib al-maqdur fi akhbar Taymur, c. 1435.
Ruy Gonzalez de Clavijo. Embajada a Tamorlan, 1406.
Manz, Beatrice Forbes. The Rise and Rule of Tamerlane. Cambridge University Press, 1989.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Timur's campaigns are characterized even in friendly chronicles by the practice of building towers from the skulls of defeated populations (kelle-menara). Reliable estimates place deaths from Timurid campaigns at 15-17 million or roughly 5% of the world's population — one of the highest ratios in history (Matthew White, Great Big Book of Horrible Things, 2011, synthesizing Clavijo, Ibn Arabshah, and Yazdi). The 1398 sack of Delhi massacred ~100,000 prisoners in a single day (Zafarnama admits this) and reportedly depopulated the city for a generation. Isfahan massacre (1387): ~70,000 killed. Baghdad massacre (1401): ~90,000 killed. His piety as a Muslim sovereign — he cultivated ulama and Sufi orders — did not temper the violence against other Muslims.

title: dictator
description: Roman general, consul, and dictator. Conquered Gaul (58-50 BCE), crossed the Rubicon in 49 BCE launching civil war against the Pompeian faction, defeated Pompey at Pharsalus (48 BCE), installed Cleopatra VII in Egypt (47 BCE), crushed remaining Optimates at Thapsus (46 BCE) and Munda (45 BCE). Named dictator perpetuo in February 44 BCE, assassinated on the Ides of March by 60 senators led by Brutus and Cassius.
notable_events: -44 assassination on the Ides of March

sources.citation: Gaius Julius Caesar. De Bello Gallico, c. 52 BCE; De Bello Civili, c. 46 BCE.
Suetonius. Divus Iulius, in De Vita Caesarum, c. 120 CE.
Plutarch. Life of Caesar, c. 110 CE.
Goldsworthy, Adrian. Caesar: Life of a Colossus. Yale University Press, 2006.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Caesar's own De Bello Gallico reports ~1 million Gauls killed and ~1 million enslaved during the conquest — figures corroborated by Plutarch. Modern scholars (Goldsworthy 2006; Henige in 'He Came, He Saw, We Counted: The Historiography and Demography of Caesar's Gallic Numbers,' 1998) treat these as order-of-magnitude correct given the sustained eight-year campaign across a population of 4-5 million. The 55 BCE massacre of the Usipetes and Tencteri (Caes. BG 4.14-15) killed women and children across the Rhine; even Cato demanded Caesar be turned over to the Germans as a war criminal. The 52 BCE siege of Uxellodunum ended in the mutilation of all defenders who bore arms. These acts were genocidal by modern standards.

title: emperor (princeps)
description: First Roman emperor and founder of the Julio-Claudian dynasty. Grand-nephew and adopted heir of Julius Caesar. Triumvir (43-33 BCE) with Mark Antony and Lepidus, then defeated Antony and Cleopatra at Actium (31 BCE). Given the title Augustus in 27 BCE, he established the Principate while preserving republican forms. His 41-year reign inaugurated the Pax Augusta. Annexed Egypt, Noricum, Raetia, Moesia, Pannonia, and pushed frontiers to the Rhine and Danube.
notable_events: -43 Second Triumvirate, proscriptions that killed Cicero
-27 grant of the title Augustus, Principate begins
9 CE Teutoburg Forest disaster, three legions annihilated under Varus

sources.citation: Augustus. Res Gestae Divi Augusti, 14 CE.
Suetonius. Divus Augustus.
Cassius Dio. Roman History, books 45-56.
Eck, Werner. The Age of Augustus. Blackwell, 2003.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The 43 BCE proscriptions under the Second Triumvirate formally condemned ~300 senators and 2,000 equites to death with property confiscation; Cicero, whose hands and head were displayed on the Rostra, was Octavian's price for alliance with Antony. The Perusine War (41-40 BCE) ended in Octavian reportedly executing 300 Italian notables on the altar of Divus Julius (Suet. Aug. 15). The Res Gestae is propaganda that suppresses these events. The Principate's 'restoration of the republic' was a fiction that constitutionalized monarchy.

title: emperor
description: First Christian Roman emperor. Proclaimed augustus by his troops at York (Eboracum, 306) after his father Constantius Chlorus's death. Defeated Maxentius at the Milvian Bridge (312) — reportedly under the sign of the chi-rho. Sole emperor from 324 after defeating Licinius. Issued the Edict of Milan (313, joint with Licinius) legalizing Christianity; presided over the First Council of Nicaea (325) against Arianism; founded Constantinople as the new eastern capital in 330.

sources.citation: Eusebius of Caesarea. Vita Constantini, c. 339 CE.
Lactantius. De Mortibus Persecutorum, c. 315 CE.
Barnes, Timothy D. Constantine and Eusebius. Harvard University Press, 1981.
Lenski, Noel (ed.). The Cambridge Companion to the Age of Constantine. Cambridge University Press, 2012.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Constantine's execution of his eldest son Crispus (326) and his wife Fausta (killed by scalding in an overheated bath, ostensibly on suspicion of incest) is poorly explained by hagiographic sources; Eusebius omits the incident. Barnes (1981) argues it was a political purge. The conversion narrative of 312 was first reported by Eusebius decades later; whether Constantine was religiously convinced or politically opportunistic is unresolved (Lenski 2012 surveys positions). The Donatist controversy he suppressed in Africa involved military coercion. His elevation of Christianity set the template for medieval Christendom's suppression of pagans, Jews, and heretics.

title: emperor
description: King of the Franks from 768, king of the Lombards from 774, and the first emperor in the West since 476, crowned by Pope Leo III on Christmas Day 800. Over 30 years of almost continuous warfare subdued the Saxons (772-804), Lombards (774), Bavaria (788), Avars (791-796), and the Spanish March (778-801). Patron of the Carolingian Renaissance, establishing a palace school at Aachen under Alcuin of York.
notable_events: 800 imperial coronation by Pope Leo III at St Peter's

sources.citation: Einhard. Vita Karoli Magni, c. 829.
Royal Frankish Annals (Annales regni Francorum), 8-9th c.
McKitterick, Rosamond. Charlemagne: The Formation of a European Identity. Cambridge University Press, 2008.
Nelson, Janet L. King and Emperor: A New Life of Charlemagne. Penguin, 2019.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The 32-year Saxon Wars constitute one of early medieval Europe's most systematic campaigns of forced conversion. The Capitulatio de partibus Saxoniae (c. 785) prescribed death for refusal of baptism, for violation of Christian funerary rules, and for any 'pagan' practice. The 782 Massacre of Verden — in which the Annals record the beheading of 4,500 Saxon rebels in a single day on Charlemagne's order — is sometimes doubted but is reported in two independent annalistic traditions. Mass deportations from Saxony to Frankish territory continued into 804. The Carolingian Renaissance's literary achievements were built on the back of this violence.

title: emperor
description: Holy Roman Emperor, King of Sicily, King of Germany, King of Jerusalem. Raised in multilingual Palermo (spoke German, Sicilian, Latin, Greek, Arabic). Negotiated the 1229 recovery of Jerusalem without battle via treaty with Sultan al-Kamil during the Sixth Crusade — and was excommunicated by Pope Gregory IX twice for the attempt and for his perceived heterodoxy. Promulgated the 1231 Constitutions of Melfi, one of medieval Europe's most advanced legal codes.
notable_events: 1241 victory over Guelphs at Faenza; Mongols invade nearby

sources.citation: Liber Augustalis / Constitutiones regni Siciliae, 1231.
Matthew Paris. Chronica Majora, 13th c.
Kantorowicz, Ernst. Kaiser Friedrich der Zweite. Berlin, 1927.
Abulafia, David. Frederick II: A Medieval Emperor. Oxford University Press, 1988.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Frederick's reputation for religious tolerance is qualified. He deported the entire Sicilian Muslim population (~30,000-60,000) to Lucera in Apulia 1224-1226 and forbade them Islamic practice outside the colony. Under his successors Lucera was destroyed in 1300 and the population deported to slavery or massacred. His heresy codes (1224, 1231, 1238-1239) prescribed death by burning for convicted Cathars and established the legal template later used by the Inquisition. Revisionist readings (Abulafia 1988) rejected the Kantorowicz hagiography of Frederick as proto-modern secular prince.

title: queen regnant
description: Queen of Castile and León, co-sovereign with her husband Fernando II of Aragón (the Reyes Católicos / Catholic Monarchs). Presided over the 1492 trifecta: conquest of Granada (end of eight-century Iberian Muslim rule), expulsion of Jews (Alhambra Decree, 31 March 1492 — ~200,000 expelled), and Columbus's first voyage. Established the Tribunal of the Holy Office of the Inquisition by papal bull of 1478, with the first auto-da-fé at Seville in 1481.

sources.citation: Hernando del Pulgar. Crónica de los Reyes Católicos, c. 1490.
Andrés Bernáldez. Memorias del reinado de los Reyes Católicos, c. 1500.
Liss, Peggy K. Isabel the Queen: Life and Times. Oxford University Press, 1992.
Kamen, Henry. The Spanish Inquisition: A Historical Revision. Yale University Press, 1997.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002/007: The Inquisition under Isabel killed ~2,000 by burning in the first 20 years (Kamen 1997) and persecuted tens of thousands more — especially conversos (former Jews). The 1492 Alhambra Decree expelled ~100,000-200,000 Jews, a foundational act of modern European antisemitism. The 1502 forced conversion edict dissolved the 1492 Granada surrender terms guaranteeing Muslim religious freedom; crypto-Muslims (Moriscos) were eventually expelled 1609-1614. Under Isabel Columbus began the genocide of the Taíno — he was rebuked by the Crown for enslavement (Bobadilla investigation 1500) but the encomienda system was subsequently legalized. The Catholic Monarchs title was granted by Borgia pope Alexander VI.

title: queen regnant
description: Queen of England and Ireland, last Tudor monarch. Daughter of Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn. Established the Elizabethan Religious Settlement (1559) codifying a moderate Protestantism. Defeated the Spanish Armada (1588). Presided over the flowering of English theatre (Shakespeare, Marlowe), the first joint-stock chartered companies (Muscovy 1555, Levant 1581, East India 1600), the plantations of Ireland (Munster 1586, Ulster 1609-10 under successor), and the privateering wars against Spain.
notable_events: 1559 Act of Supremacy and Act of Uniformity
1587 execution of Mary Queen of Scots

sources.citation: John Stow. Annales, or a Generale Chronicle of England, 1615.
William Camden. Annales Rerum Anglicarum et Hibernicarum Regnante Elizabetha, 1615.
Guy, John. Elizabeth: The Forgotten Years. Viking, 2016.
Somerset, Anne. Elizabeth I. Knopf, 1991.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Nine Years' War in Ireland (1594-1603) under Lord Mountjoy used systematic starvation as strategy; Fynes Moryson, Mountjoy's secretary, records deliberate destruction of harvests producing famine. Edmund Spenser's A View of the Present State of Ireland (c. 1596) explicitly advocated the strategy. Sir Humphrey Gilbert's 1569 pacification of Munster decorated the path to his tent with the heads of executed Irish — a tactic Elizabethan officers adopted broadly. The Munster Plantation (1586) dispossessed ~300,000 acres of Irish lands. Elizabeth licensed John Hawkins's slave-trading voyages (1562, 1564, 1567) — England's first documented participation in the transatlantic slave trade. The Royal African Company (1660) was later, but Elizabeth's imprimatur on Hawkins was the precedent.

title: emperor
description: Corsican-born artillery officer who rose through the French Revolutionary armies. First Consul from 1799 (coup of 18 Brumaire), Emperor of the French from 1804. At his apogee (1810-12) dominated continental Europe. Defeated decisively at Leipzig (1813) and finally at Waterloo (1815); died in exile on Saint Helena.

sources.citation: Correspondance de Napoléon Ier (32 vols.), 1858-1870.
Las Cases. Le Mémorial de Sainte-Hélène, 1823.
Roberts, Andrew. Napoleon: A Life. Viking, 2014.
Tulard, Jean. Napoléon ou le mythe du sauveur. Fayard, 1977.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Napoleonic Wars killed ~3-6 million soldiers and civilians (Esdaile 2007). Re-imposition of slavery in French colonies (Law of 20 May 1802) reversed the 1794 Convention abolition; enforcement in Guadeloupe under Richepanse and in Saint-Domingue under Leclerc and Rochambeau was genocidal — Rochambeau's 1803 use of mass drownings and dog-hunts of rebels is documented. Mulatto/Black officers like Alexandre Dumas (the novelist's father) were purged from the army. The 1808 Egyptian Institute looting, the Spanish guerra de independencia (~500,000 deaths), the Russian campaign (~500,000 Grande Armée casualties out of 685,000 who crossed the Niemen). Simultaneous legacy: Napoleonic Code, metric system, meritocratic civil service. Both must be documented.

title: empress
description: Empress of Russia 1762-1796. German princess (Sophie of Anhalt-Zerbst) who seized the throne in a coup against her husband Peter III. Over 34 years expanded Russia by ~520,000 km² through the partitions of Poland (1772, 1793, 1795) and wars with the Ottomans (annexation of Crimea 1783). Corresponded with Voltaire and Diderot; legislated the 1767 Nakaz; but crushed the Pugachev Rebellion (1773-1775) with atrocity and extended serfdom into Ukraine.
notable_events: 1783 annexation of Crimea (end of Crimean Khanate)

sources.citation: Catherine II. Memoirs (written c. 1750s-70s, published 1859).
Madariaga, Isabel de. Russia in the Age of Catherine the Great. Yale University Press, 1981.
Alexander, John T. Catherine the Great: Life and Legend. Oxford University Press, 1989.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002/004: Catherine's 'Enlightened Absolutism' coexisted with the extension of hereditary serfdom (krepostnoe pravo) to the newly annexed Ukrainian and Polish lands — affecting ~10 million people. Her 1785 Charter to the Nobility confirmed nobles' absolute rights over serfs including sale without land. The Pugachev suppression killed tens of thousands and was followed by Pugachev's quartering in Moscow (1775). The three partitions of Poland-Lithuania erased the Commonwealth as a sovereign state. The 1783 annexation of Crimea broke treaty guarantees to the khanate and initiated decades of displacement of Crimean Tatars to Ottoman territory (completed 1944 by Stalin). Her 1792 withdrawal of support from the Polish constitutional reformers enabled the Targowica partition.

title: queen (empress of India from 1876)
description: Queen of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland. Inherited the throne aged 18; reigned 63 years, the second-longest in British history. Given the title Empress of India in 1876 following Disraeli's Royal Titles Act. Her reign corresponded with the apex of British imperial expansion: annexation of Punjab (1849), Oudh (1856), Burma (three wars ending 1886), Hong Kong (1842), much of Africa (1884-1900), and the suppression of the 1857 Indian Uprising.
notable_events: 1857-58 Indian Uprising suppression, dissolution of East India Company

sources.citation: Queen Victoria's Journals (published RCIN 100000-). Royal Archives.
Letters of Queen Victoria, 3 series, 1907-1932.
Baird, Julia. Victoria: The Queen. Random House, 2016.
Hibbert, Christopher. Queen Victoria: A Personal History. HarperCollins, 2000.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002/004: Victoria's constitutional role was not decorative; her diaries and correspondence show active intervention (opposing Gladstone's Irish Home Rule, supporting the Zulu and Anglo-Boer wars). Under her reign: the 1857-58 suppression of the Indian Uprising killed ~800,000 Indians (Dalrymple, The Last Mughal); the 1845-52 Irish Famine under her early reign killed 1 million with another 1 million emigrating while British landlords continued grain exports; the 1887-1900 Indian famines killed ~15-29 million (Mike Davis, Late Victorian Holocausts, 2001) under laissez-faire policies enforced by her governors-general; the Second Anglo-Boer War concentration camps killed ~28,000 Boer women and children and ~20,000 Black inhabitants (1900-02). Victoria personally opposed racial equality ('half-castes' in her correspondence) but kept an Indian secretary Abdul Karim.
dynasty: Hanover (House of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha from 1840)

title: emperor
description: Eastern Roman (Byzantine) emperor. Native speaker of Latin — the last Latin-speaker on the Byzantine throne. Codified Roman law in the Corpus Iuris Civilis (529-534) that became the basis for civil-law traditions worldwide. Reconquered Italy from the Ostrogoths (535-554), North Africa from the Vandals (533-534), and parts of Spain from the Visigoths. Built the Hagia Sophia (532-537) and hundreds of other structures.

sources.citation: Procopius of Caesarea. De Bellis; De Aedificiis; Historia Arcana, mid-6th c.
John Malalas. Chronographia, 6th c.
Evans, J. A. S. The Age of Justinian. Routledge, 1996.
Kaldellis, Anthony. Procopius of Caesarea: Tyranny, History, and Philosophy at the End of Antiquity. Penn, 2004.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Gothic War (535-554) devastated Italy — Procopius reports Rome reduced to 500 inhabitants at one point. The reconquest was undone within a generation (Lombard invasion 568). The 532 Nika Riots were suppressed by Belisarius and Mundus herding rioters into the Hippodrome and massacring ~30,000. Procopius's Anekdota (Secret History, c. 550) — the most scathing insider portrait of any Roman emperor — accused Justinian of embezzlement and of causing population declines in the millions; Kaldellis (2004) treats it as largely factual if rhetorically overheated. Justinian's legal code suppressed pagans (529 Athens Academy closure), Jews, Samaritans, and theological dissenters.

title: emperor
description: Byzantine emperor known as 'Rhinotmetos' ('cut-nosed') after his mutilation and first deposition in 695. Restored to the throne in 705 with the help of the Bulgar Khan Tervel. Pursued a policy of mass resettlement of Slavs and Cypriots; his heavy taxation and purges during his second reign (705-711) led to a coup in which he was beheaded and his family killed.
notable_events: 705 restoration with Bulgar aid, massacre of opponents

sources.citation: Theophanes the Confessor. Chronographia, early 9th c.
Nikephoros. Breviarium Historicum, late 8th c.
Head, Constance. Justinian II of Byzantium. University of Wisconsin Press, 1972.

ethical_notes: Justinian II's reputation in sources written under the Isaurian dynasty that overthrew his line is especially hostile. The rhinotomy was the Byzantine alternative to execution — a mutilation intended to make one unfit to rule; his restoration despite it set a precedent. His second reign's executions of rival families and forced resettlement of Cyprus were acts of political terror.

title: king
description: First Bourbon king of France. King of Navarre from 1572, king of France from 1589 via the Salic Law after the extinction of the Valois. Converted from Calvinism to Catholicism ('Paris vaut bien une messe,' perhaps apocryphal) in 1593 to pacify Paris. Issued the 1598 Edict of Nantes granting limited Huguenot toleration. Assassinated by the Catholic zealot Ravaillac in 1610.
notable_events: 1572 St Bartholomew's Day massacre (spared only because royal)
1598 Edict of Nantes; Treaty of Vervins

sources.citation: Sully. Mémoires ou Oeconomies royales, 1639.
Pierre de l'Estoile. Journal pour le règne de Henri IV, c. 1610.
Babelon, Jean-Pierre. Henri IV. Fayard, 1982.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: The Edict of Nantes — often romanticized as 'tolerance' — was a pragmatic peace ending the Wars of Religion, not a principle of religious freedom; it was revoked in 1685 by Louis XIV. The French Wars of Religion (1562-1598) killed 2-4 million (Knecht). Henri IV's own 1572 marriage to Marguerite de Valois triggered the St Bartholomew's Day massacre in which Catholic forces killed 5,000-30,000 Huguenots across France. His 1598 edict did not apply to New France, where Huguenot settlers were later forbidden.

title: tsar (emperor from 1721)
description: Tsar of All Russia from 1682 (co-tsar with Ivan V until 1696), first Emperor of All Russia from 1721. Pursued radical westernization: built St Petersburg from scratch on the Neva swamps (founded 1703) with an estimated 100,000+ conscript deaths; reformed army and navy on European models; defeated Charles XII of Sweden at Poltava (1709), ending Swedish great-power status. Imposed the beard tax and Western dress on the boyars.
notable_events: 1721 Treaty of Nystad, adoption of imperial title
1721 abolition of patriarchate; Holy Synod replaces it

sources.citation: Correspondence and Papers of Peter the Great (Pis'ma i bumagi imp. Petra Velikogo), 13 vols., 1887-2003.
Massie, Robert K. Peter the Great: His Life and World. Knopf, 1980.
Hughes, Lindsey. Russia in the Age of Peter the Great. Yale University Press, 1998.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Peter's personal torture and execution of his son Alexei (1718), on suspicion of plotting with Austria, included supervising sessions where Alexei was knouted to death. The 1698 Streltsy suppression executed ~1,200 personally — Peter participated in the executions. Construction of St Petersburg on the malarial Neva floodplain used serf and convict labor; mortality estimates range 30,000-200,000. The Great Northern War (1700-1721) killed ~350,000 Russians. Russian Orthodox schismatics (Old Believers) were persecuted with taxation, burning, and forced resettlement. The institution of the soul tax (1718-24) formalized serfdom's fusion with state power.

title: king
description: King of Prussia, Elector of Brandenburg, third Hohenzollern king. Militarized the Prussian state; seized Silesia from Maria Theresa in 1740 launching the War of Austrian Succession; survived the Seven Years' War (1756-1763) against Austria, France, Russia, and Sweden largely through the 'Miracle of the House of Brandenburg' (Tsarina Elisabeth's death, 1762). Patron of Voltaire and the Berlin Academy, composer of flute concertos.

sources.citation: Frederick II. Œuvres de Frédéric le Grand (30 vols), 1846-57.
Blanning, Tim. Frederick the Great: King of Prussia. Penguin, 2015.
Clark, Christopher. Iron Kingdom: The Rise and Downfall of Prussia 1600-1947. Allen Lane, 2006.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The 1740 invasion of Silesia was an unprovoked war of aggression launched the same year Frederick took the throne — Clark (2006) calls it the decisive act that 'inaugurated the era of cabinet wars' and the Prussian-Austrian antagonism that structured German history for 126 years. The Seven Years' War killed ~1.4 million; Prussia lost ~180,000 soldiers and perhaps 500,000 civilians died in the eastern provinces from war, famine, and plague. Frederick's antisemitic General Privilege of 1750 classified Jews into restricted categories with punishing residence taxes. His enlightened correspondence with Voltaire sat alongside a cabinet of racial-hierarchy theorists.

name_regnal: Roi des Belges, Souverain de l'État indépendant du Congo
title: king
description: Second king of the Belgians and, as private sovereign 1885-1908, absolute ruler of the Congo Free State. At the 1884-85 Berlin Conference obtained personal sovereignty over ~2.3 million km² in central Africa under the pretext of antislavery humanitarianism. Enforced rubber and ivory quotas through the Force Publique, which committed systematic atrocities. Annexed by Belgium in 1908 after international outrage (Casement Report 1904, Congo Reform Association).
notable_events: 1908 forced transfer of Congo to Belgian state

sources.citation: Roger Casement. The Casement Report, UK Parliamentary Papers, 1904.
Mark Twain. King Leopold's Soliloquy, 1905.
Hochschild, Adam. King Leopold's Ghost: A Story of Greed, Terror and Heroism in Colonial Africa. Houghton Mifflin, 1998.
Vansina, Jan. Being Colonized: The Kuba Experience in Rural Congo, 1880-1960. University of Wisconsin Press, 2010.
Stanard, Matthew G. Selling the Congo. University of Nebraska Press, 2011.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002/004: Leopold's Congo Free State killed an estimated 5-10 million Congolese between 1885 and 1908 (Hochschild 1998 cites Vansina and demographic modeling suggesting ~10 million, or ~50% of the pre-colonial population). Enforcement of rubber quotas used hostage-taking of women, amputation of hands (of living and dead) as proof of ammunition expenditure, village burnings, and systematic rape. The Force Publique's corporal punishment was the chicotte — a rhinoceros-hide whip. Leopold personally profited ~220 million gold francs (~$1.1 billion 2020); he used it to build the Arcade du Cinquantenaire and Royal Museum for Central Africa (Tervuren). His statue still stood at Brussels in 2020 before protests — Belgian government continues to refuse an official apology for the genocide. ANY representation of Leopold that calls this 'colonial administration' rather than genocide is historically false.

title: grand prince (knyaz)
description: Grand Prince of Kievan Rus'. Son of Svyatoslav I and the Varangian concubine Malusha. Christianized the Rus' by personal conversion (c. 988, traditionally on marriage to Byzantine princess Anna Porphyrogenita) and enforced baptism of Kiev's population. In Orthodox tradition venerated as Vladimir the Great / St Vladimir.
notable_events: 980 seizure of Kiev after killing brother Yaropolk
996 consecration of the Church of the Tithes

sources.citation: Primary Chronicle (Povest' vremennykh let), c. 1113.
John Skylitzes. Synopsis Historion, 11th c.
Franklin, S. and Shepard, J. The Emergence of Rus 750-1200. Longman, 1996.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: The Primary Chronicle portrays pre-conversion Vladimir as a brutal polygamous pagan who murdered his brother Yaropolk and compelled the widow of Yaropolk to marriage — the hagiographic reversal after baptism is a Christian trope. The 988 conversion was enforced on the Kievan population under duress; sources describe idols being dragged behind horses and thrown in the Dnieper, and ongoing conflict with pagan resistance movements for two centuries (especially in Novgorod and the northeast). Modern Ukrainian historians emphasize that the Kyivan Rus' state is a Ukrainian, not exclusively Russian, heritage — a contested point since Russia's 2022 invasion.

title: queen regnant
description: Queen regnant of Castile 1504-1555 and of Aragon 1516-1555, mother of Emperor Charles V. Daughter of Isabella I and Fernando II. Called 'La Loca' ('the Mad') because her father (until 1516) and her son (from 1509) imprisoned her at Tordesillas on claims of mental incapacity to rule in her name. Spent 46 years under house arrest, during which neither her father nor her son allowed her public sovereignty.
notable_events: 1504 accession to Castilian throne on Isabel's death
1516 succession in Aragon on Fernando's death; Carlos arrives Spain 1517

sources.citation: Archivo General de Simancas, Patronato Real, papers on Juana.
Aram, Bethany. Juana the Mad: Sovereignty and Dynasty in Renaissance Europe. Johns Hopkins University Press, 2005.
Dennis, Amarie. Seek the Darkness: The Story of Juana la Loca. Anaya, 1969.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: Modern historiography (Aram 2005) argues the 'madness' label was politically manufactured by her father Fernando (to retain Castile) and her son Carlos (to assume rule of both crowns) — not a medical fact. Portuguese and Habsburg sources diverge on her mental state. The 46-year confinement at Tordesillas is one of the longest documented politically motivated imprisonments of a reigning queen in European history. The Comuneros (1520-21) rose in her name demanding her liberation; Carlos V suppressed them. ETHICS note: calling her 'La Loca' without qualification reproduces the succession-politics smear.

title: duchess and queen consort
description: Duchess of Aquitaine and Gascony in her own right from 1137. Queen of France (as wife of Louis VII 1137-1152), then queen of England (as wife of Henry II 1152-1189); mother of Richard I and John of England. Participated in the Second Crusade (1147-1149). After Henry's death acted as effective regent for her crusading son Richard I; negotiated his 1194 ransom from Emperor Henry VI.
notable_events: 1137 accession as duchess, marriage to Louis VII
1173-74 'Revolt of the Eaglets' — imprisoned by Henry II until 1189

sources.citation: John of Salisbury. Historia Pontificalis, c. 1163.
Richard of Devizes. Chronicon de tempore regis Ricardi Primi, c. 1192.
Turner, Ralph V. Eleanor of Aquitaine: Queen of France, Queen of England. Yale University Press, 2009.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: Medieval chronicles often misogynistically attribute Eleanor's political activity to sexual scandal — the most egregious being the unfounded Antioch-incest-rumors from chroniclers hostile to Louis VII's marriage. Her actual constitutional role as Duchess of Aquitaine (a territory larger than the royal domain of France) is often overlooked. Her imprisonment 1173-1189 by Henry II was a textbook case of a medieval 'rebellious queen' suppressed.

title: king
description: Capetian king of France, 43-year reign that transformed France from a weak feudal kingdom into Europe's leading monarchy. Confiscated the continental Plantagenet domains in the 1202-1214 war with King John (Normandy, Anjou, Maine, Touraine, Poitou). Victory at Bouvines (27 July 1214) over the Imperial-English coalition. Paris became the administrative capital.

sources.citation: Rigord and Guillaume le Breton. Gesta Philippi Augusti, early 13th c.
Baldwin, John W. The Government of Philip Augustus. University of California Press, 1986.
Bradbury, Jim. Philip Augustus, King of France 1180-1223. Longman, 1998.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Philippe's 1182 expulsion of Jews from royal domains and confiscation of their property, re-invited in 1198 under punitive taxation, set the medieval template later imitated by Edward I (1290) and Charles VI (1394). He authorized the Albigensian Crusade (1209-1229) which killed ~200,000-1,000,000 in Languedoc and destroyed Cathar civilization; though Simon de Montfort led operations, Philippe's refusal to intervene or restrain was deliberate. His 1214 Bouvines victory consolidated a French nation-state but also set the template for royal-centralized tax extraction.

title: king
description: Grand Duke of Lithuania from 1377, King of Poland from 1386 as husband of the 11-year-old queen-regnant Jadwiga of Anjou. Last pagan king of Lithuania — his 1386 baptism at Kraków and Union of Krewo brought Lithuania into Western Christendom. Led the Polish-Lithuanian victory at Grunwald / Tannenberg (15 July 1410), breaking Teutonic Order expansion in the Baltic. Founder of the Jagiellonian dynasty that would rule Poland-Lithuania until 1572.

sources.citation: Jan Długosz. Annales seu Cronicae incliti Regni Poloniae, 15th c.
Rowell, S. C. Lithuania Ascending: A Pagan Empire within East-Central Europe 1295-1345. Cambridge University Press, 1994.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: Jagiello's 1386 baptism enabled the forced conversion of Lithuania, the last pagan state in Europe; Samogitia resisted until 1413. Baltic pagan sanctuaries were destroyed. The Polish-Lithuanian Union, while enabling defensive cooperation, laid groundwork for 16th-c. polonization of the Lithuanian and Ruthenian nobility. Queen Jadwiga, his 11-year-old wife, was rewritten in later hagiography as a free agent; the 1386 marriage was dynastic coercion.

title: king
description: King of Hungary and Croatia; after 1469 contested king of Bohemia. Elected by the Hungarian Diet at 15 following the death of László V. Built the Black Army (Fekete Sereg), one of Europe's first standing professional armies. Briefly conquered Moravia, Silesia, and Lower Austria (captured Vienna 1485). Founded the Bibliotheca Corviniana, one of Renaissance Europe's largest libraries, of which only fragments survive.
notable_events: 1463 recovery of the Holy Crown from Frederick III

sources.citation: Antonio Bonfini. Rerum Ungaricarum Decades, 1488-1496.
Engel, Pál. The Realm of St Stephen: A History of Medieval Hungary 895-1526. I.B. Tauris, 2001.

ethical_notes: Matthias's reign is popularly remembered in Hungarian tradition as a golden age ('Mátyás király meghalt, oda az igazság' — 'King Matthias died, justice with him'), but it was also ruinously taxed to fund the Black Army; peasant rebellions in the 1490s after his death (culminating in Dózsa 1514) had roots in his extractive fiscal reform. His wars against Bohemia and Austria were dynastic aggression without strategic necessity.

title: king
description: First king of united Italy (17 March 1861). Previously king of Sardinia-Piedmont from 1849 after his father Carlo Alberto's abdication. With Cavour's statecraft and Garibaldi's military contributions, led the unification of Italy in four stages: Lombardy (1859), Central Italy (1860), the Two Sicilies (1860), Venetia (1866), and Rome (1870). Retained regnal number II (not I) to emphasize dynastic continuity with Savoy — a break from unified-Italy logic.
notable_events: 1861 proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy
1866 Third War of Independence / annexation of Venetia
1870 capture of Rome, final breccia di Porta Pia

sources.citation: Denis Mack Smith. Italy: A Modern History. University of Michigan Press, 1959.
Duggan, Christopher. Francesco Crispi: From Nation to Nationalism. Oxford University Press, 2002.
Di Fiore, Gigi. Controstoria dell'unità d'Italia. Rizzoli, 2007.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Risorgimento narrative obscures southern atrocities. The 1861-1870 'brigantaggio' war in the former Kingdom of the Two Sicilies killed more Italians than all Risorgimento battles combined — estimated 20,000-150,000 (Pinto 2007, Di Fiore 2007 in revisionist Sud-focused historiography). Pontelandolfo and Casalduni (August 1861) were destroyed by Italian troops in retaliation for bandit raids, 400+ killed. Mass emigration from the Mezzogiorno followed the economic destruction of the south's proto-industry by free-trade integration with the north. The 'unification' was a Piedmontese annexation with a plebiscitary veneer.

title: king
description: King of Sweden, the 'Lion of the North'. Transformed the Swedish army into a standing professional force using combined-arms tactics that revolutionized early modern warfare. Entered the Thirty Years' War in 1630 landing in Pomerania, won Breitenfeld (1631) reversing Catholic ascendancy, but died at Lützen (1632). Expanded Swedish empire over the Baltic (Livonia, Estonia, parts of Finland and Poland).

sources.citation: Sveriges Riksdags Handlingar (Diet records, 17th c.).
Roberts, Michael. Gustavus Adolphus: A History of Sweden 1611-1632. Longman, 1953-58.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Thirty Years' War killed ~20% of the German population (Parker 1997); Swedish armies were as destructive of civilians as Imperial Catholic ones. Magdeburg (May 1631), though sacked by Tilly before Gustavus arrived, resulted in ~20,000 civilian deaths — a Swedish propaganda coup but a Protestant atrocity by Protestant failure to relieve. Swedish military administration in occupied Germany used systematic requisitioning that amounted to ethnic cleansing of Slavic Pomeranians and Wendish populations in the eastern Baltic.

name_regnal: Archidux Austriae, Regina Hungariae et Bohemiae
title: archduchess (effective sovereign)
description: Archduchess of Austria, Queen of Hungary and Bohemia, de facto ruler of the Habsburg domains 1740-1780. Only female ruler of the Habsburg hereditary lands. Defended her inheritance in the War of the Austrian Succession (1740-48) against Frederick II's Silesia invasion. Initiated rationalist reforms (compulsory primary education 1774, abolition of judicial torture 1776) but also expelled Jews from Prague (1744-45) and doubled down on Counter-Reformation confessionalism.

sources.citation: Briefe der Kaiserin Maria Theresia an ihre Kinder und Freunde, 4 vols., 1881.
Beales, Derek. Enlightenment and Reform in Eighteenth-Century Europe. I.B. Tauris, 2005.
Ingrao, Charles W. The Habsburg Monarchy 1618-1815. Cambridge University Press, 2000.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: Maria Theresa's personal antisemitism is well documented — her 1744-45 expulsion of ~20,000 Prague Jews on military-collaborator pretext was the largest Jewish expulsion in 18th-c. Europe; ultimately rescinded under British and Dutch diplomatic pressure but she maintained heavy 'Jew taxes' (Judensteuer) throughout her reign. Her Counter-Reformation policy persecuted Protestants in Hungary and Bohemia. She participated in the First Partition of Poland (1772) against her own stated principles ('I weep but I take' — reported in Frederick II's correspondence). The portrait of 'enlightened despotism' obscures these.

title: king
description: Founder of the Akkadian Empire, the first known empire in world history. Of obscure origin (the 'Sargon Legend' describes him as a foundling raised by a water-drawer). Seized power at Kish, conquered Sumer, extended rule to the Mediterranean coast (Ebla campaigns), the Zagros mountains, and Elam. Appointed his daughter Enheduanna high priestess of Nanna at Ur — she is the earliest known named author in world literature.
notable_events: -2334 seizure of Kish, foundation of Akkad dynasty
-2300 campaigns to the Cedar Forest and Silver Mountain

sources.citation: Sargon Birth Legend, Neo-Assyrian copy (Nineveh), 7th c. BCE.
Cursing of Agade, Sumerian literary text, c. 2100 BCE.
Liverani, Mario. The Ancient Near East. Routledge, 2014.
Westenholz, Joan Goodnick. Legends of the Kings of Akkade. Eisenbrauns, 1997.

name_variants.name: Šarru-ukīn
name_variants.context: Akkadian reading 'the king is true' — throne name

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: Sargon's reign is reconstructed from hostile Sumerian tradition (Cursing of Agade blamed his grandson Naram-Sin for causing a famine by sacrilege) and from Neo-Assyrian propaganda that claimed descent from Akkad ~1500 years later. The historicity of his conquests is secure in outline but specific battles are legendary. Akkadian was imposed as the administrative language over Sumerian — the first documented language shift by imperial fiat.

title: king
description: Sixth king of the First Dynasty of Babylon. Initially ruled a small city-state; by his 30th regnal year had conquered Larsa, Eshnunna, Mari, and Assyria's dependencies, unifying Mesopotamia. Promulgated the Code of Hammurabi — 282 laws inscribed on a diorite stele now in the Louvre, one of the oldest deciphered legal codes (though predated by Ur-Nammu, c. 2100 BCE).
notable_events: -1763 conquest of Larsa under Rim-Sin I

sources.citation: Code of Hammurabi stele, c. 1754 BCE, Louvre Museum Sb 8.
Letters from Mari, 18th c. BCE corpus (Archives royales de Mari, 1950-).
Van De Mieroop, Marc. King Hammurabi of Babylon: A Biography. Blackwell, 2005.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: The Code of Hammurabi is frequently celebrated as proto-human-rights, but its 282 provisions embed a rigid three-class system (awīlum, muškēnum, wardum) with different punishment schedules; the same offense brought death for a slave and a fine for a freeman. Lex talionis applied only within social class. Provisions on women, slaves, and debtors were harsh by any standard — including the death penalty for a tavern-keeper who gave shelter to fugitives (§109). Van De Mieroop (2005) treats the 'code' as royal propaganda rather than practical law.

title: king of kings
description: Founder of the Achaemenid Persian Empire. Achaemenid king of Anshan from 559 BCE; overthrew his Median suzerain Astyages (550), defeated Croesus of Lydia (547/546), captured Babylon (539) and freed the exiled Judaeans. Established a precedent of tolerant imperial administration — allowed subject peoples to keep their religions, languages, and local rulers under satrapal oversight.
notable_events: -550 defeat of Astyages, end of Median empire

sources.citation: Cyrus Cylinder (British Museum ME 90920), c. 538 BCE.
Herodotus. Historiai, book 1, c. 440 BCE.
Xenophon. Cyropaedia, c. 370 BCE.
Kuhrt, Amélie. The Persian Empire: A Corpus of Sources from the Achaemenid Period. Routledge, 2007.
Briant, Pierre. Histoire de l'empire perse. Fayard, 1996.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: The Cyrus Cylinder is frequently misrepresented (notably in 20th-c. Pahlavi propaganda and at the UN) as 'the first declaration of human rights'. Kuhrt (2007) and Briant (1996) argue it is a conventional Mesopotamian foundation inscription of a conquering king, asserting divine legitimacy via Marduk — not a proclamation of universal rights. The 'tolerance' of Achaemenid rule was also real, but contingent on submission and tribute. The 547 Lydian campaign and 539 Babylon conquest involved routine ancient warfare; Herodotus describes Croesus nearly burned on a pyre.

title: king of kings
description: Third Achaemenid Great King. Seized the throne in 522 BCE after killing Gaumata/Bardiya (as he narrates it in the trilingual Behistun Inscription). Re-established imperial control after widespread revolts, expanded the empire to its maximum extent at his death. Organized the empire into 20 satrapies, reformed taxation, coined the Daric, built the royal road, and initiated the Greco-Persian Wars (500-490 BCE, ending at Marathon).
notable_events: -522 seizure of throne, killing of Bardiya
-513 campaign across the Danube against the Scythians

sources.citation: Behistun Inscription, c. 519 BCE, Kermanshah.
Herodotus. Historiai, books 3-6.
Briant, Pierre. Histoire de l'empire perse. 1996.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: The Behistun Inscription is a royal self-justification; modern scholars (Briant 1996) see the story of 'Gaumata the Magus' as probable propaganda masking Darius's usurpation from a legitimate Bardiya. The 'twenty satrapies' system institutionalized imperial extraction; Herodotus 3.89-97 tabulates annual tribute. The suppression of the Ionian Revolt (499-493) included the sack of Miletus (494) and enslavement of its population.

title: pharaoh (queen)
description: Seventh and last active Ptolemaic ruler of Egypt. Ruled jointly with her brothers-husbands Ptolemy XIII (51-47) and Ptolemy XIV (47-44) and then with her son Ptolemy XV Caesarion (44-30). The only Ptolemaic ruler known to have spoken Egyptian (Plutarch Ant. 27). Allied with Caesar against Ptolemy XIII in the Alexandrine War (48-47), later with Mark Antony. Defeated at Actium (31 BCE) and died in 30 BCE; Octavian annexed Egypt.
notable_events: -48 arrival of Pompey, assassination on Egyptian beach
-41 alliance with Mark Antony at Tarsus
-30 death by suicide; annexation of Egypt

sources.citation: Plutarch. Life of Antony, c. 110 CE.
Cassius Dio. Roman History, books 42-51.
Roller, Duane W. Cleopatra: A Biography. Oxford University Press, 2010.
Schiff, Stacy. Cleopatra: A Life. Little Brown, 2010.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-004: Cleopatra's historical portrayal — from Octavian's propaganda (Augustus cultivated the 'foreign seductress' trope to justify Actium as a war against Egypt not a Roman civil war) to Shakespeare to Hollywood — has been persistently Orientalized and racialized. The Ptolemies were ethnically Macedonian Greeks who ruled Egypt as a colonial elite; her contemporary portraits show a Hellenistic queen, though coins emphasized her Egyptian religious role as pharaoh. Debate over her 'Blackness' often ignores Ptolemaic Macedonian-Greek identity. Her suicide by asp (Plutarch) may be apocryphal; Roller (2010) considers poison more likely.

title: prophet / political leader
description: Prophet of Islam and political founder of the first Islamic polity. Of the Hashim clan of Quraysh, orphaned young, worked as merchant; received prophetic revelations from 610 CE. Emigrated from Mecca to Yathrib (Medina) in 622 — the Hijrah — establishing the first Muslim community (Ummah) under the Constitution of Medina. Conquered Mecca in 630. At death in 632 the Arabian peninsula was largely unified under Islam.
notable_events: 627 Battle of the Trench; siege and execution of Banu Qurayza

sources.citation: Quran (c. 610-632, canonized c. 650 under Uthman).
Ibn Ishaq. Sirat Rasul Allah, c. 767 (transmitted via Ibn Hisham 833).
Al-Tabari. Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk, c. 915.
Watt, W. Montgomery. Muhammad at Mecca (1953); Muhammad at Medina (1956). Oxford.
Donner, Fred M. Muhammad and the Believers: At the Origins of Islam. Harvard, 2010.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: Muslim tradition treats Muhammad's biography with theological weight; Western academic sira study (Watt, Donner) treats it as historical but source-critical. The 627 execution of 600-900 men of the Jewish Banu Qurayza tribe after the Battle of the Trench is reported by Ibn Ishaq and al-Tabari; scholarship divides on whether all accounts are historical, partial, or later literary elaboration (Arafat 1976, Ahmad 1979, Kister 1986). The captured families were enslaved. The Hudaybiyyah treaty and the conquest of Mecca (630, largely bloodless) show statesmanship; the wars of apostasy (ridda, 632-633 under Abu Bakr) immediately after his death were the state's enforcement of political Islam. Atlas records must present both the hagiographic Muslim tradition and the critical academic tradition.

title: caliph
description: Second of the Rashidun (Rightly-Guided) caliphs. Converted to Islam c. 616; became caliph on the death of Abu Bakr in 634. In his 10-year reign Muslim armies conquered Sasanian Persia (Qadisiyyah 636, Nahavand 642), the Levant (Yarmouk 636), and Egypt (Alexandria 641). Established the diwan registry, the Hijri calendar dating from 622, and the institution of the caliphate as a distinct office.
notable_events: 644 assassination by Persian slave Abu Lu'lu'a Firuz

sources.citation: Al-Tabari. Tarikh, 10th c.
Al-Baladhuri. Futuh al-Buldan, 9th c.
Donner, Fred. The Early Islamic Conquests. Princeton University Press, 1981.
Hoyland, Robert G. In God's Path: The Arab Conquests and the Creation of an Islamic Empire. Oxford, 2015.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Early Muslim Conquests killed hundreds of thousands and destroyed the Sasanian Empire as a polity (though non-Muslim Persians — Zoroastrians, Jews, Christians — survived under the dhimma pact). Jerusalem's surrender (638) was peaceful; other cities resisted and were sacked. Umar's Covenant (al-'Uhda al-'Umariyya), whether authentic or later elaborated, codified dhimmi restrictions including the jizya tax, prohibitions on new church construction, required clothing distinctions, and riding restrictions — the legal template for ~1,300 years of second-class status for non-Muslims under Islamic rule. Non-Muslim sources (Coptic, Jewish, Armenian) record both the relief at escaping Byzantine/Sasanian oppression and the new constraints.

title: caliph
description: Fifth Abbasid caliph. Reign 786-809 coincided with the Abbasid apogee in Baghdad: patronage of scholarship (the nascent Bayt al-Hikmah developed under his son al-Ma'mun), the 1001 Nights frame narrative (a literary construction), diplomatic exchanges with Charlemagne (including the elephant Abul-Abbas sent to Aachen 802). Eliminated the Barmakid family of viziers in 803 to concentrate power.

sources.citation: Al-Tabari. Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk, 10th c.
Al-Ya'qubi. Tarikh, 9th c.
Einhard. Vita Karoli Magni, 9th c. (Frankish source).
Kennedy, Hugh. When Baghdad Ruled the Muslim World. Da Capo, 2004.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: The 1001 Nights Harun is a literary construct; the historical caliph enforced heavy taxation that provoked revolts (Khurasan 806, the Yahya ibn Abdallah al-Zaydi rising). The Barmakid purge (803) — in which Ja'far was beheaded and the family dispossessed — remains contested; Kennedy (2004) sees it as concentration of finance in caliphal hands after a decade of Barmakid delegation. Abbasid slave economy was vast (zanj East African slaves imported to Iraqi marshes led to the 869-883 Zanj Revolt).

title: sultan
description: Kurdish founder of the Ayyubid dynasty. Nephew of Shirkuh, with whom he served Nur ad-Din of Mosul in the Fatimid-Egypt campaigns. Abolished the Fatimid caliphate in Egypt (1171). Sultan of Egypt and Syria from 1174. Decisively defeated the Crusader army of Guy of Lusignan at the Horns of Hattin (4 July 1187); reconquered Jerusalem (2 October 1187) after 88 years of Crusader rule. Defended against the Third Crusade (1189-1192) without losing Jerusalem.
notable_events: 1192 Treaty of Ramla with Richard I

sources.citation: Baha' ad-Din ibn Shaddad. al-Nawadir al-Sultaniyya wa'l-Mahasin al-Yusufiyya, c. 1195.
Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani. al-Fath al-Qussi fi'l-Fath al-Qudsi, c. 1192.
William of Tyre. Historia rerum in partibus transmarinis gestarum, c. 1184.
Ehrenkreutz, Andrew S. Saladin. SUNY Press, 1972.
Eddé, Anne-Marie. Saladin. Harvard University Press, 2011.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-004: The European romantic tradition from Dante (who placed Saladin in Limbo, not Hell) through Walter Scott (The Talisman, 1825) idealized Saladin as 'chivalrous Muslim'. Arabic sources portray him as devout but politically calculating. Post-Hattin he executed the Templars and Hospitallers (200 knights) but spared the ordinary infantry (sold or ransomed). The 1187 surrender of Jerusalem was negotiated with Balian of Ibelin; Muslim tradition dwells on the contrast with the 1099 Crusader massacre. His family wars with the Zangids and ruthlessness to the Fatimid heirs (mass imprisonment) are often overlooked.

title: sultan
description: Tenth Ottoman sultan, longest reigning (46 years), presided over the apogee of Ottoman power. Conquered Belgrade (1521), Rhodes (1522), defeated Hungary at Mohács (1526), besieged Vienna (1529), conquered Baghdad from the Safavids (1534) and annexed most of North Africa. Legal reformer — his reforms earned him the sobriquet 'Kanuni' (the Lawgiver) in Ottoman tradition.

sources.citation: Mustafa Ali. Künhü'l-ahbar, late 16th c.
Ogier Ghiselin de Busbecq. Legationis Turcicae Epistolae, 1589.
Inalcik, Halil. The Ottoman Empire: The Classical Age 1300-1600. Phoenix, 1973.
Kafadar, Cemal. Between Two Worlds: The Construction of the Ottoman State. UC Press, 1995.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Süleyman ordered the execution of his son Şehzade Mustafa (1553) by imperial mutes via bowstring, widely attributed to the intrigue of his consort Hürrem Sultan; this broke the Ottoman succession norm and inaugurated a destabilizing pattern of princely fratricide. His sieges were ruinous — Szigetvár's defenders (1566) were massacred. Balkan devshirme (Christian boy-levy for janissary and palace service) continued under him, affecting tens of thousands annually — described in Christian sources as a blood tax (haraç), though Ottoman sources present it as opportunity. The 1541 conquest of Buda converted the main Hungarian church to a mosque, and Hungarian Christian populations were reduced to haraçguzar status.

title: pharaoh
description: Fifth pharaoh of the Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt. Widow of Thutmose II; initially regent for her stepson Thutmose III, assumed full pharaonic titulature by year 7 of his nominal reign. Reigned c. 1479-1458 BCE — one of the longest reigns of any female ruler of ancient Egypt. Commissioned the Djeser-Djeseru mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahari (designed by Senenmut), and the Punt Expedition (c. 1477 BCE) whose reliefs document the first fully illustrated naval trade mission in world history.
notable_events: -1458 death; later damnatio memoriae under Thutmose III

sources.citation: Deir el-Bahari mortuary temple reliefs, c. 1470 BCE.
Turin Canon (Ramesside king list, c. 1250 BCE).
Roehrig, Catharine H. (ed.). Hatshepsut: From Queen to Pharaoh. Metropolitan Museum of Art, 2005.
Tyldesley, Joyce. Hatchepsut: The Female Pharaoh. Viking, 1996.
