ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: The damnatio memoriae of Hatshepsut under her stepson Thutmose III (defacement of monuments, erasure from king lists) was long attributed to personal revenge; modern scholarship (Roehrig 2005) treats it as politically motivated around year 42+ of Thutmose III's reign, to legitimize his son Amenhotep II's succession — not a misogynistic erasure contemporary to her reign. Her depiction with the royal beard in reliefs is iconographic pharaonic legitimacy, not (as earlier 19th-c. scholarship suggested) male impersonation.

title: pharaoh
description: Third pharaoh of the Nineteenth Dynasty. Reigned 66 years, one of the longest reigns in Egyptian history. Fought the Hittites under Muwatalli II at the Battle of Kadesh (1274 BCE) — the first battle for which detailed tactical records survive; concluded the first known peace treaty in history (Treaty of Kadesh, 1259 BCE). Built monumental complexes at Abu Simbel, Pi-Ramesse (the new delta capital), Karnak, and the Ramesseum.

sources.citation: Poem of Pentaur and Kadesh Bulletins, c. 1274 BCE reliefs at Karnak, Luxor, Abydos, Abu Simbel.
Treaty of Kadesh tablets, Akkadian and Egyptian versions, 1259 BCE.
Kitchen, Kenneth A. Pharaoh Triumphant: The Life and Times of Ramesses II. Aris & Phillips, 1982.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: The Kadesh reliefs present Ramesses's solo heroic rescue from Hittite encirclement as a triumph; the reality documented by the Hittite archives (Boğazköy tablets) and by the Treaty of Kadesh text itself is a tactical Egyptian near-disaster followed by a strategic draw. The peace treaty of 1259 BCE is both sides' acknowledgment that neither could prevail. Ramesses's practice of usurping the monuments of predecessors (especially Merneptah's father Seti I and further back) complicates chronological attribution of Nineteenth Dynasty works.

title: king/pharaoh
description: Kushite king of Napata who conquered Egypt in ~728 BCE, founding the Twenty-fifth ('Nubian' or 'Kushite') Dynasty. His 44-line Victory Stele at Gebel Barkal recounts the northward campaign — one of ancient Egypt's most detailed military narratives, written entirely in Egyptian (Piye was bilingual Nubian-Egyptian). The Kushites reunified Egypt after the fragmented Third Intermediate Period.
notable_events: -727 formal submission of Lower Egyptian princes

sources.citation: Piye Victory Stele (Gebel Barkal), c. 727 BCE, Cairo Museum JE 48862.
Manetho. Aegyptiaca (fragments), 3rd c. BCE.
Török, László. The Kingdom of Kush. Brill, 1997.
Morkot, Robert. The Black Pharaohs: Egypt's Nubian Rulers. Rubicon, 2000.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-004: The 25th Dynasty's identification as 'Black Pharaohs' (Morkot 2000) is accurate — Piye, Shabaka, Shebitku, Taharqa, and Tantamani were Nubian (from modern Sudan). 19th-20th c. Western Egyptology systematically downplayed Kushite-Egyptian ruler status as 'alien' or 'intrusion' despite Piye's explicit Egyptianizing piety (restoring temples, worshipping Amun at Karnak). The Assyrian conquest 671-663 BCE by Esarhaddon and Ashurbanipal expelled the 25th Dynasty, who retreated to Napata and later Meroe (see kingdom of Kush / Meroe entities).

title: mansa (emperor)
description: Tenth mansa (ruler) of the Mali Empire. Famously pilgrimaged to Mecca in 1324 via Cairo, giving away so much gold in Egypt that the Cairo gold market collapsed and did not recover for a decade (Al-Umari, Masalik al-absar, 1347). Traveled with ~60,000 attendants and ~80 camel-loads of gold dust. Sponsored the construction of the Djinguereber and Sankoré mosques and madrasas at Timbuktu under the Andalusian architect as-Sahili, and Gao's Great Mosque.
notable_events: 1325 patronage of Timbuktu architecture on return

sources.citation: Shihab al-Din al-Umari. Masalik al-absar fi mamalik al-amsar, c. 1347.
Ibn Battuta. Rihla, account of his 1352 visit to Mali.
Ibn Khaldun. Kitab al-'Ibar, c. 1377.
Levtzion, Nehemia. Ancient Ghana and Mali. Methuen, 1973.
Gomez, Michael A. African Dominion: A New History of Empire in Early and Medieval West Africa. Princeton University Press, 2018.

ethical_notes: Mansa Musa's wealth — often cited as 'richest person in history' in 21st-c. popular media — rests on gold-quantity calculations that cannot be verified precisely. The 1324 hajj disrupted Cairo's monetary system in ways Egyptian sources considered disastrous for ordinary merchants. Slavery was central to Malian wealth extraction — trans-Saharan slave trade operated under and through his empire; al-Umari notes he took slaves as gifts. The Timbuktu mosques and scholarly establishment are his most enduring legacy.

title: emperor
description: 225th Emperor of Ethiopia in the Solomonic dynasty tradition. Ras Tafari Makonnen, regent from 1916, crowned emperor in 1930 as Haile Selassie I. His famous 1936 appeal to the League of Nations against Mussolini's invasion (occupation 1935-1941) is a founding document of collective security discourse. Restored 1941 with British assistance. Modernized the state but fell to the Derg in 1974; murdered in 1975.
notable_events: 1963 founding of the Organisation of African Unity in Addis Ababa

sources.citation: Haile Selassie I. My Life and Ethiopia's Progress (autobiography), 1937 and 1976.
Marcus, Harold G. Haile Sellassie I: The Formative Years. University of California Press, 1987.
Zewde, Bahru. A History of Modern Ethiopia 1855-1991. James Currey, 2001.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Haile Selassie's modernization rested on an underlying feudal order; the 1960-73 Wollo and Tigray famines killed ~200,000+ while the court concealed the scale (Jonathan Dimbleby's 1973 documentary 'The Unknown Famine' precipitated the 1974 revolution). Land was not redistributed. Dissent from students and Oromo nationalists was suppressed. His personal role in the 1960 attempted coup suppression and in the 1962 Eritrean federation dissolution (making Eritrea a province, triggering the 30-year war) is documented by Zewde (2001). The Rastafarian movement's veneration of him as divinity is a religious-diasporic phenomenon he publicly rejected.

title: emperor
description: Emperor of Ethiopia 1889-1913. King of Shewa from 1865. Defeated the Italian invasion at the Battle of Adwa (1 March 1896) — the decisive military defeat of a European colonial army by an African state during the Scramble for Africa. Nearly doubled the territory of the Ethiopian Empire through southward conquests (Oromo territories, Sidamo, Kaffa) that remain controversial in Ethiopian historiography. Introduced electricity, telegraph, postal service, and founded Addis Ababa (1886).
notable_events: 1889 Treaty of Wuchale (ambiguity over Article 17 led to war)
1908 stroke, de facto end of personal rule

sources.citation: Wallis Budge, E. A. A History of Ethiopia, Nubia and Abyssinia. Methuen, 1928.
Marcus, Harold G. The Life and Times of Menelik II. Oxford University Press, 1975.
Bulatovich, Aleksandr. Ethiopia Through Russian Eyes, trans. Seltzer. Red Sea Press, 2000.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002/004: The Adwa victory is rightly celebrated as an anti-colonial triumph; simultaneously Menelik's southern conquests (1882-1898) were themselves imperial expansion. Oromo populations (the Arsi massacre of 1886 by Ras Gobena and Menelik's forces killed ~5,000-10,000 in the Annole and Chelenqo campaigns); Kaffa's king Tato Gaki Sherocho was deported in 1897 and the kingdom annihilated; Sidamo, Gedeo, and Wolayta were incorporated with significant violence and subsequent feudal land alienation to northern colonists (neftenya). The Ethiopian Empire's multi-ethnic composition and the ongoing Oromo question have roots in this period. Modern Oromo historiography (Tesema Ta'a, Asafa Jalata) calls it 'Abyssinian colonialism' — a label contested by Amhara-centric traditions.

title: mansa (founder)
description: Founder of the Mali Empire. Defeated the Sosso king Sumanguru Kante at the Battle of Kirina (c. 1235) unifying the Mandinka clans into the Manden Kurufaba (Mande Federation). Ibn Khaldun (Kitab al-'Ibar) records him as 'Mari Jata, the greatest of them'. His reign is primarily preserved through the Epic of Sundiata (Sunjata Fasa) oral tradition of the Mandinka jeli (griots) — one of West Africa's defining epics.
notable_events: c. 1240 conquest of Niani and Old Ghana

sources.citation: Ibn Khaldun. Kitab al-'Ibar, c. 1377.
Niane, Djibril Tamsir. Sundiata: An Epic of Old Mali. Longman (English trans. 1965, from oral performance by Mamadou Kouyaté).
Conrad, David C. Empires of Medieval West Africa. Chelsea House, 2005.
Gomez, Michael A. African Dominion. Princeton, 2018.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-003 (uncertainty transparency): Sundiata's historicity is well-supported (Ibn Khaldun provides an independent Arabic source), but specific biographical details (childhood paralysis, exile, hunter allies) come from oral tradition with centuries of transmission; date ranges (1217-1255) are scholarly reconstructions with ±10 year confidence. The Manden Charter of Kurukan Fuga — orally transmitted and proclaimed by UNESCO as intangible heritage (2009) — is sometimes claimed as a pre-modern declaration of rights; the academic consensus (Gomez 2018) is that extant form reflects ongoing oral-tradition elaboration over 800 years, not a single 1235 document.

title: emperor
description: Founder of the Maurya Empire. Of disputed origin (Mauryan tradition: Moriya kshatriya; Buddhist Mahavamsa: Moriya clan; Jain tradition: humble origin). Guided by the political strategist Chanakya/Kautilya, overthrew the Nanda dynasty at Pataliputra (c. 322 BCE). Defeated the Seleucid satrap Seleucus I Nicator in the Seleucid-Mauryan war (305-303 BCE), acquiring the trans-Indus provinces in exchange for 500 war elephants. Traditionally abdicated c. 297 BCE to become a Jain ascetic, dying by ritual fast (sallekhana) at Shravanabelagola.

sources.citation: Kautilya. Arthashastra (attribution disputed, 3rd c. BCE - 1st c. CE).
Megasthenes. Indica (fragments preserved in Strabo, Arrian, Diodorus), c. 300 BCE.
Mahavamsa, 5th c. CE (Pali Buddhist chronicle).
Thapar, Romila. Aśoka and the Decline of the Mauryas. Oxford, 1961; rev. 2012.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-003: Chandragupta's biography is largely legendary. The synchronism with Seleucus's reign (305 BCE) and Megasthenes's embassy (c. 302 BCE) establishes his historical reality, but earlier details depend on later Buddhist (Mahavamsa), Jain (Parishishta-Parva), and Brahmanical (Puranic) traditions that disagree on caste and pre-imperial history. The Arthashastra's ruthless Realpolitik — advocating torture, assassination, and sexual entrapment — has been contested as to whether it reflects Mauryan practice or later idealization (Trautmann 1971).

title: emperor
description: Third Mauryan emperor. Under his reign (c. 268-232 BCE) the empire reached its maximum extent covering almost the entire Indian subcontinent except the Chola-Pandya-Chera south. The Kalinga War (c. 261 BCE) is described in his own Major Rock Edict 13 as having killed 100,000 and enslaved 150,000 — after which he embraced Buddhist dhamma and disseminated edicts across the empire in Prakrit, Greek, and Aramaic. His edicts call for moral governance, religious tolerance, and compassion to animals; he sent Buddhist missions to Sri Lanka, Gandhara, and the Hellenistic kingdoms.

sources.citation: Ashokan Edicts (Rock, Pillar, Cave inscriptions), c. 268-232 BCE — 40+ extant locations.
Mahavamsa, 5th c. CE.
Ashokavadana, 2nd c. CE (Sanskrit Buddhist narrative).
Thapar, Romila. Aśoka and the Decline of the Mauryas. 2012.
Olivelle, Patrick (ed.). Ashoka in Ancient India. Harvard, 2017.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Ashoka's self-reported Kalinga casualties (100,000 killed, 150,000 deported) are in his own Major Rock Edict 13 — a remarkable royal admission of wartime atrocity. His post-Kalinga conversion to dhamma (not narrow Buddhism — the edicts speak to a pluralist ethics) is one of history's most extensively self-documented royal moral transformations. However, Olivelle (2017) and other scholars caution against mapping modern Indian nationalist 'Ashoka as proto-secular' idealization onto the actual edicts, which endorse Brahmanical social order alongside Buddhist ethics. The Ashokavadana hostile stories (great slaughterer before conversion, burned 18,000 Ajivikas) are likely later Buddhist embellishment.

title: emperor
description: Third Gupta emperor, during whose reign classical Sanskrit culture reached its apogee. Conquered the Western Kshatrapas of Malwa and Gujarat (c. 400 CE), opening the Arabian Sea trade. His court at Ujjain is traditionally credited with hosting the 'Nine Jewels' (Navaratna) including Kalidasa (poet/dramatist), Varahamihira (astronomer), and Dhanvantari (physician) — though synchronisms are disputed. Contemporary with the Chinese Buddhist pilgrim Faxian, whose Foguoji records an ordered, prosperous India.

sources.citation: Mehrauli Iron Pillar inscription, c. 400 CE.
Faxian. Foguoji / Record of Buddhist Kingdoms, c. 414 CE.
Gupta coinage (gold dinara issues).
Majumdar, R.C. (ed.). The Classical Age (History and Culture of the Indian People vol. 3). Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan, 1954.
Willis, Michael. The Archaeology of Hindu Ritual: Temples and the Establishment of the Gods. Cambridge, 2009.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-004: The 'Gupta Golden Age' is a 19th-20th c. nationalist construct partly built to claim a Hindu-Sanskrit classical analog to Greek-Roman Europe (Jha 2002). It obscures continuing caste/jati stratification documented in contemporary Dharmashastra texts, the decline of Buddhist institutions under Brahmanical patronage, and the exclusion of women and Shudras from Sanskrit literary culture. Chandragupta II's military campaigns included the routine destruction of rival royal houses.

title: emperor
description: Third Mughal emperor, grandson of Babur. Took the throne aged 13 after the death of Humayun; ruled 49 years. Expanded the empire over most of the Indian subcontinent north of the Godavari. Abolished the jizya tax on non-Muslims (1564), married Rajput princesses and integrated Rajputs into Mughal nobility, convened the interreligious Ibadat Khana debates (1575-), and promulgated his personal syncretic cult Din-i-Ilahi (1582). Illiterate by his own admission but patron of translation projects and a vast court library.
notable_events: 1556 Second Battle of Panipat (under regent Bairam Khan)

sources.citation: Abu'l-Fazl ibn Mubarak. Akbarnama, 1590-96.
Abu'l-Fazl. Ain-i-Akbari, c. 1595.
Abd al-Qadir Bada'uni. Muntakhab al-Tawarikh, c. 1595-96 (hostile counter-narrative).
Richards, John F. The Mughal Empire. Cambridge University Press, 1993.
Truschke, Audrey. The Language of History: Sanskrit Narratives of Muslim Pasts. Columbia, 2021.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Akbar's reputation for tolerance is substantially earned — the Ibadat Khana debates included Jains, Parsis, Jesuits, and Shia-Sunni scholars; the jizya abolition had no Islamic parallel before. BUT the 1568 Siege of Chittor ended in a general massacre of ~30,000 non-combatants on Akbar's order, as recorded in the Akbarnama itself — including the slaughter of peasants who had refused to submit. Bada'uni's Muntakhab al-Tawarikh (written secretly) accuses Akbar of abandoning orthodox Islam for sun-worship and celebrated his death. The Din-i-Ilahi never had more than ~19 adherents and died with him. The 1603 Battle of Ahmadnagar destruction of Daulatabad complex killed thousands.

title: emperor
description: Sixth Mughal emperor, reign 1658-1707. Won a four-way war of succession against his brothers, executing Dara Shikoh (1659) and imprisoning his father Shah Jahan at the Agra Fort. Expanded the empire to its maximum extent (~4 million km² at apogee) through the Deccan campaigns (1682-1707), annexing Bijapur (1686) and Golconda (1687). Reintroduced jizya on non-Muslims (1679). His 49-year reign ended amid the drawn-out Deccan war, endemic peasant rebellions (Jats, Satnamis, Sikhs, Marathas), and fiscal exhaustion.
notable_events: 1658-59 War of Succession, execution of Dara Shikoh
1675 execution of Sikh Guru Tegh Bahadur

sources.citation: Sa'id-ullah Khan (Munshi). Ahkam-i-Alamgiri, c. 1710.
Niccolao Manucci. Storia do Mogor, 1705.
Jadunath Sarkar. History of Aurangzib. M.C. Sarkar, 5 vols., 1912-1930.
Truschke, Audrey. Aurangzeb: The Man and the Myth. Penguin, 2017.
Richards, John F. The Mughal Empire. 1993.

name_variants.name: Aurangzeb
name_variants.context: standard English (from Persian 'Aurangzeb' 'ornament of the throne')

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002/004: Aurangzeb is perhaps the most contested ruler in Indian historiography. Documented facts: reimposed jizya (1679); demolished the Keshava Deo Temple at Mathura (1670), the Vishwanath Temple at Varanasi (1669), the Somnath Temple (1665); executed the ninth Sikh Guru Tegh Bahadur (1675) for refusing conversion; systematically recruited Hindus to senior mansabdari positions at historically high rates (Truschke 2017 citing Athar Ali's data). Hindutva historiography (V.D. Savarkar, Jadunath Sarkar) portrays him as a straightforward religious persecutor; revisionist scholarship (Truschke 2017, Richards 1993, Athar Ali 1966) notes his selective temple destruction targeted political enemies and his increased Hindu mansabdar recruitment above Akbar-era levels. Both framings capture partial truths. His personal austerity (he had the Quran copied for a living, refused silks) coexisted with ruthless political violence including imprisoning his father for seven years (Shah Jahan died at the Agra Fort in 1666).

title: emperor
description: Founder of the Mughal Empire. Timurid prince of Andijan (Ferghana), descended from Timur on his father's side and Genghis Khan on his mother's. Lost Samarkand three times, then invaded India via the Khyber Pass; won the First Battle of Panipat against Ibrahim Lodi (21 April 1526) with Ottoman-style gunpowder tactics (cannon + match-lock arquebus), establishing Mughal rule. Author of the Baburnama (Chagatai Turkic memoirs) — one of the earliest and most vivid autobiographies by any Asian ruler.
notable_events: 1527 Battle of Khanwa (defeat of Rajput confederation)
1528 destruction of the Babri Masjid site (disputed — see ethical_notes)

sources.citation: Babur. Baburnama (Tuzuk-i-Baburi), c. 1527-1530.
Gulbadan Begum. Humayunnama, c. 1587.
Dale, Stephen F. The Garden of the Eight Paradises: Babur and the Culture of Empire in Central Asia, Afghanistan and India (1483-1530). Brill, 2004.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Babur's reputation in India is overshadowed by the 1992 destruction of the Babri Masjid at Ayodhya, which Hindutva organizations argued had been built over the Ram Janmabhoomi by Babur's commander Mir Baqi in 1528. Modern academic consensus (Supreme Court of India 2019 judgment acknowledging the 2003 ASI survey) is that the mosque was built on earlier Hindu structures; whether Babur personally ordered the temple's destruction is unproven in the Baburnama (the relevant folios are lost). The campaigns themselves — in his own memoirs — include boasts about skull-pyramids after Bajaur (1519, ~3,000 killed) and the 1527 Khanwa battle where prisoners were 'counted' as decapitated heads. His memoirs are simultaneously literary and unflinching about these.

title: chhatrapati (emperor-equivalent)
description: Founder of the Maratha Empire. Born at Shivneri; killed the Bijapur general Afzal Khan at Pratapgad (1659) with the 'wagh nakh' tiger claws. Attacked Shaista Khan's camp at Pune (1663), escaping the Mughal siege by disguising as a basket of sweets (1666). Crowned Chhatrapati at Raigad (6 June 1674) reviving Hindu sovereignty in the Deccan after four centuries of Muslim rule. Built a professional navy, the Mawala infantry, and a fortified hill-fort network that survived him by 140 years.
notable_events: 1659 killing of Afzal Khan at Pratapgad
1660 sack of Shaista Khan's camp at Pune

sources.citation: Sabhasad Bakhar (Marathi chronicle), 1697.
Jedhe Shakavali, 17th c. chronology.
English East India Company Surat factory records, 1660s.
Sarkar, Jadunath. Shivaji and His Times. 5th ed. M.C. Sarkar, 1952.
Gordon, Stewart. The Marathas 1600-1818. Cambridge University Press, 1993.
Laine, James W. Shivaji: Hindu King in Islamic India. Oxford University Press, 2003.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-004/007: Shivaji has been politically instrumentalized by Hindu-nationalist movements (Shivsena, BJP) in 20th-21st c. Maharashtra in ways that distort the historical figure. Laine's 2003 academic biography was banned in Maharashtra and the Bhandarkar Institute (where Laine had done research) was sacked by protesters over nuanced treatment of Shivaji's family; this illustrates the risks of historical writing on figures politically canonized. Historical Shivaji: his wars were regional Deccan politics against the Adil Shahis, Qutb Shahis, and Mughals more than a proto-nationalist 'Hindu' struggle; his administration employed Muslims (e.g. Siddi Hilal, Ibrahim Khan); the 1664 sack of Surat targeted English and Dutch establishments and killed Indian civilians. The Surat loot was reportedly worth 10 million rupees.

title: maharaja
description: First Maharaja of the Sikh Empire. Unified the twelve Sikh misls into a single state; coronation at Lahore (12 April 1801). Blind in one eye from smallpox. Annexed Lahore (1799), Amritsar (1802), Multan (1818), Kashmir (1819), Peshawar (1823, annexed 1834). Built a modern army (Fauj-i-Khas) trained by French and Italian officers from Napoleon's dissolved Grande Armée including Jean-François Allard and Jean-Baptiste Ventura. The empire ended within a decade of his death in the two Anglo-Sikh wars (1845-46, 1848-49).

sources.citation: Sohan Lal Suri. Umdat-ut-Tawarikh, 19th c. (Persian).
Diwan Amarnath. Zafar Namah-i-Ranjit Singh, 1836.
Grewal, J. S. The Sikhs of the Punjab. Cambridge University Press, 1990.
Singh, Khushwant. Ranjit Singh: Maharaja of the Punjab. Penguin, 2008.

ethical_notes: NOTE on entity_name: no matching entity for the Sikh Empire / Sarkar Khalsa exists in AtlasPI's current roster — this is an atlas gap. 'Krolestwo Polskie' is an obvious misfit; the true mapping should be created. Ranjit Singh's reign was religiously inclusive (Sikh Maharaja with Hindu and Muslim ministers including Fakir Azizuddin and Diwan Dina Nath), suppressed sati in most of Punjab, but his raid-based expansion sacked Afghan and Kashmiri cities. Treatment of Kashmiri Muslims after the 1819 annexation was oppressive (Walter Lawrence's 1895 Settlement Report documents the consequences). The Koh-i-Noor diamond he extracted from Shah Shuja in 1813 was later seized by the British in 1849 and remains in the Tower of London (a diplomatic grievance between India, Pakistan, and the UK).

title: king
entity_name: ការ្យ​ ​សីលេន
description: King of the Khmer Empire. Seized the throne c. 1113 by killing his great-uncle Dharanindravarman I. Commissioned Angkor Wat, the largest religious monument in the world, as a Vaishnavite mortuary temple identified with himself as Paramavishnuloka ('gone to the supreme abode of Vishnu'). Led multiple campaigns against Đại Việt (Ly dynasty) in 1128, 1132, 1138 — all repelled; contemporary Vietnamese sources (Dai Viet Su Ky Toan Thu) record these as Khmer invasions. Attacked the Cham kingdom of Champa (1145) occupying Vijaya.

sources.citation: Angkor Wat inscriptions, 12th c.
Lê Văn Hưu. Đại Việt Sử Ký, 1272 (earliest Vietnamese dynastic history, lost but transmitted).
Coedes, George. The Indianized States of Southeast Asia. East-West Center, 1968.

name_variants.name: សូរ្យវរ្ម័នទី២

ethical_notes: Suryavarman's reign exemplifies the Khmer devaraja tradition that absorbed massive hydraulic and laboring resources to build temples identifying the king with deity. Angkor Wat's construction employed ~300,000 laborers over 30+ years (Fletcher 2012 LIDAR studies); this was slave-adjacent corvée labor within a status hierarchy. His wars with Champa and Dai Viet killed tens of thousands.

title: king
entity_name: ការ្យ​ ​សីលេន
description: King of the Khmer Empire. Seized the throne after defeating the 1177-1181 Cham invasion of Angkor. Commissioned massive public works: Angkor Thom walled city with the Bayon temple (famous for its 216 giant face-stones), Ta Prohm and Preah Khan monastic complexes, 102 hospitals, and 121 rest-houses along the royal roads. First Angkor king to make Mahayana Buddhism the state religion. Campaigns reconquered Champa (1190s) and briefly made it a Khmer vassal.

sources.citation: Preah Khan stele inscription, 1191.
Ta Prohm stele inscription, 1186.
Zhou Daguan. Zhenla Fengtu Ji (Record of Cambodia), c. 1297 (post-reign).
Jacques, Claude. Angkor: Cities and Temples. River Books, 1997.

name_variants.name: ជ័យវរ្ម័នទី៧

ethical_notes: Jayavarman VII's hospital/rest-house network (documented on the Preah Khan and Ta Prohm inscriptions, which also list 97,840 temple personnel) is sometimes idealized as proto-welfare-state; the institutions served the king's religious merit rather than an egalitarian ethic. His monumental building strained the state's hydrological system; by the 14th c. Angkor was in demographic decline, a process modern LIDAR studies (Greater Angkor Project 2007-) associate with climate stress compounded by over-engineered water infrastructure.

title: king
description: Fourth king of Majapahit. Reigned during the empire's apogee under the statecraft of Mahapatih (Prime Minister) Gajah Mada. The Nagarakretagama (1365) by the court poet Mpu Prapanca describes Majapahit's territorial claims covering most of modern Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, southern Thailand, and southern Philippines — though effective control outside Java is debated. The 1357 Bubat Incident, in which Gajah Mada massacred a Sundanese royal wedding party that refused to accept subordinate status, scarred inter-Javanese relations.

sources.citation: Mpu Prapanca. Desawarnana (Nagarakretagama), 1365.
Pararaton, early 16th c. Javanese chronicle.
Kitab Kidung Sundayana, 16th c. Sundanese narrative of Bubat.
Noorduyn, J. and Teeuw, A. Three Old Sundanese Poems. KITLV, 2006.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-004: The Majapahit 'maximum' territory claims of the Nagarakretagama (a panegyric, not a gazetteer) have been used by 20th-c. Indonesian nationalist ideology (notably the 1928 Youth Pledge and Suharto's New Order) to frame the archipelago as historically unified under Java. This historiographical move elided Sundanese, Malay, Acehnese, Balinese, and eastern-archipelago identities. The 1357 Bubat Incident remains a point of Sundanese grievance.

title: king
description: Founder of the Majapahit Empire. Son-in-law of the last Singhasari king Kertanegara (assassinated 1292 by Jayakatwang of Kediri). Collaborated with the Yuan Chinese invasion force sent by Kublai Khan in 1293 to punish Singhasari, used them to defeat Jayakatwang, then turned on and expelled the Yuan expedition (one of the only major Mongol expeditionary failures). Founded his capital at Majapahit on the Brantas river.
notable_events: 1293 Yuan landing; joint campaign against Jayakatwang; betrayal of Yuan

sources.citation: Pararaton, early 16th c.
Kidung Panji Wijayakrama, c. 15th c.
Yuan Shi, biography of Shi Bi, c. 1370.
Reid, Anthony. A History of Southeast Asia: Critical Crossroads. Wiley, 2015.

ethical_notes: Wijaya's successful 'use and expel' strategy against the Yuan (1293) — feigning alliance, leveraging their advance on Jayakatwang, then ambushing them as they withdrew — is an early-modern textbook case of diplomatic double-cross. Majapahit's foundation was thus built on trilateral betrayal.

name_original: นเรศวรมหาราช
title: king
entity_name: อาณาจักรอยุธยา
description: King of Ayutthaya 1590-1605. After Ayutthaya's fall to the Burmese Toungoo Empire in 1569, taken as hostage to the Burmese court at Pegu. Returned to Ayutthaya in 1571, declared independence in 1584. His single combat victory over the Burmese crown prince Mingyi Swa at Nong Sarai (January 1593 in Burmese records / 18 January 1593 in the Thai narrative) is one of Thai history's most celebrated events, though details (whether a formal elephant-back duel or a chaotic battlefield encounter) are debated. Extended Ayutthaya's power over Chiang Mai (1595) and Cambodia (1594).
notable_events: 1569 Ayutthaya's fall to Burma, Naresuan taken hostage
1593 Battle of Nong Sarai (elephant duel with Mingyi Swa)

sources.citation: Luang Prasoet Chronicle (Phongsawadan Luang Prasoet), 1680.
Hmannan Yazawin (Burmese Glass Palace Chronicle), 1829-32.
Wyatt, David K. Thailand: A Short History. Yale University Press, 2003.
Ian, Sunait Chutintaranond. 'The Image of the Burmese Enemy in Thai Perceptions and Historical Writings,' Journal of the Siam Society, 1992.

name_variants.name: ဘရင်နရေရွှေ

ethical_notes: ETHICS-004: Naresuan's biography has been reshaped by 20th-c. Thai nationalist historiography (notably under Phibun Songkhram in the 1940s) to serve anti-Burmese and ethno-nationalist narratives. Revisionist scholarship (Chutintaranond 1992) notes the Thai vs Burmese framing obscures that Ayutthaya and Pegu were early-modern dynastic states with multi-ethnic populations; the 'national liberation' frame is an anachronism. The elephant duel's historicity is defended by some (the oldest chronicles, Luang Prasoet 1680, report it) and questioned by others.

title: shah
description: Last great Asian military conqueror. Turkoman Afshar tribesman who rose from brigand to commander of the Safavid restoration after the Afghan Hotaki occupation. Expelled the Afghans, defeated the Ottomans and recovered the Caucasus, then seized the throne in 1736 ending the Safavid dynasty. Invaded Mughal India (1738-39), sacked Delhi (March 1739) killing ~30,000 and looting the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor diamond. Subdued Khiva (1740), fought the Russians, and invaded Dagestan.
notable_events: 1736 deposition of Safavid Abbas III, coronation at Mughan

sources.citation: Mirza Mahdi Astarabadi. Tarikh-i Jahangusha-yi Naderi, c. 1759.
Jonas Hanway. A Historical Account of the British Trade over the Caspian Sea, 1753.
Axworthy, Michael. The Sword of Persia: Nader Shah, from Tribal Warrior to Conquering Tyrant. I.B. Tauris, 2006.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The March 1739 sack of Delhi killed ~20,000-30,000 civilians in the general massacre (qatl-e-amm) after fighting in the bazaar — Nader personally ordered the slaughter and watched from the Sunehri Masjid. Looting totaled an estimated 700 million rupees (~$700 million gold-equivalent). The Peacock Throne of Shah Jahan was dismembered and taken to Persia (destroyed 1747). The Koh-i-Noor followed the loot. His later reign descended into paranoid cruelty including the blinding of his son Reza Qoli Mirza in 1742 and mass executions of tax-defaulters, culminating in his assassination by his guards.

title: sapa inca
description: Last independent Sapa Inca of the Inca Empire. Won the Inca civil war (1529-1532) against his half-brother Huascar after the 1527-1528 smallpox epidemic (likely from Spanish contact in Panama) that killed the preceding Sapa Inca Huayna Capac and the designated heir. Defeated and captured by Francisco Pizarro at Cajamarca (16 November 1532) in an ambush on an unarmed reception retinue. Paid a ransom of one room filled with gold and two filled with silver; Pizarro executed him by garroting on 26 July 1533.
notable_events: 1532 victory at Quipaipan, capture of Huascar

sources.citation: Pedro de Cieza de León. Crónica del Perú, 1553.
Garcilaso de la Vega (el Inca). Comentarios Reales de los Incas, 1609.
Francisco de Xerez. Verdadera relación de la conquista del Perú, 1534.
Hemming, John. The Conquest of the Incas. Macmillan, 1970.
Restall, Matthew. When Montezuma Met Cortes. Ecco, 2018.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Atahualpa's death by garrote followed a farcical trial by Pizarro's men on charges of 'heresy, polygamy, fratricide (of Huascar), and inciting revolt'. The ransom room — ~11,000 kg of gold and 21,000 kg of silver — was paid in full by Inca subjects; Pizarro melted it down and divided it among his 168 men (reporting 1/5 to the Spanish crown). Pizarro should be described as a conquistador who conducted the genocide of a civilization, not as an 'explorer'. Hemming (1970) documents the Spanish conquest, smallpox epidemics (1520s and 1558-1559), labor extraction in the mita, and the indigenous population collapse from ~9 million to ~600,000 by 1620 (some estimates lower) — one of the worst demographic catastrophes in human history.

title: huey tlahtoani (emperor)
description: Ninth huey tlahtoani of Tenochtitlan and effective head of the Ēxcān Tlahtōlōyān (Aztec Triple Alliance). Centralized power by replacing regional governors with Mexica appointees. Expanded tribute collection and the flower-wars system to capture sacrificial victims. Received Hernán Cortés at Tenochtitlan on 8 November 1519; detained as a hostage; killed in June 1520 during the siege — conflicting accounts attribute his death to Mexica stoning (Spanish chronicles) or Spanish strangulation (indigenous codices including Chimalpahin).

sources.citation: Bernardino de Sahagun. Historia general de las cosas de Nueva España (Florentine Codex), c. 1569-76 (primary ethnographic source with Nahuatl informants).
Bernal Diaz del Castillo. Historia verdadera de la conquista de la Nueva España, c. 1568 (published 1632).
Codex Mendoza, c. 1541-42.
Domingo Francisco Chimalpahin Quauhtlehuanitzin. Codex Chimalpahin, early 17th c.
Restall, Matthew. When Montezuma Met Cortes: The True Story of the Meeting That Changed History. Ecco, 2018.
Clendinnen, Inga. Aztecs: An Interpretation. Cambridge University Press, 1991.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002/004: The 'Quetzalcoatl mistake' narrative — that Motecuhzoma believed Cortes was a returning god — originates in post-conquest Spanish writings (Sahagun, Diaz); it was absent from the 1519 events as reconstructed by Restall (2018), who argues it was a Spanish post-hoc fabrication to explain the conquest's success. Motecuhzoma's imprisonment was unlawful even by Spanish criteria; his death during the Mexica uprising of June 1520 — whether by stoning or by Spanish hand — precipitated the expulsion of Cortes (Noche Triste). Aztec human sacrifice was real and substantial (Clendinnen 1991 documents the ritual political economy), but Spanish numerical estimates (80,000+ sacrificed at the 1487 Templo Mayor dedication) are likely exaggerated; archaeological evidence (Lopez Lujan) supports thousands, not tens of thousands, per major ritual.

title: sapa inca
description: Ninth Sapa Inca and architect of the Tawantinsuyu (Inca) Empire. Son of Viracocha Inca; seized the throne after repelling the Chanka invasion (1438) — Viracocha had fled. Transformed Cuzco from a small chiefdom into the ritual and administrative capital of an empire that would cover ~2 million km². Commissioned Machu Picchu, Sacsayhuaman, Ollantaytambo. Reorganized the Inca state into four suyus (regions) radiating from Cuzco, imposed the mita labor draft, and established the khipu record-keeping system at imperial scale.
notable_events: 1450s conquest of the Ayarmaca and Chinchasuyu
c. 1463 shared throne with son Tupac Inca Yupanki

sources.citation: Pedro Sarmiento de Gamboa. Historia Indica, 1572 (commissioned by Viceroy Toledo; source-critical).
Juan de Betanzos. Suma y narración de los Incas, c. 1557 (married to Atahualpa's widow).
Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala. El primer nueva corónica y buen gobierno, c. 1615.
Rowe, John H. 'Inca Culture at the Time of the Spanish Conquest,' Handbook of South American Indians 2, 1946.
D'Altroy, Terence N. The Incas. Blackwell, 2002.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-003: Inca dynastic chronology derives from Spanish chronicles (Sarmiento, commissioned by Toledo to justify colonial rule by delegitimizing 'recent tyrant' Inca conquerors) and indigenous accounts (Garcilaso, Guaman Poma) that differ significantly. Pachakutiq's reign dates are approximate. The Inca imperial expansion involved routine massacres of non-submitting polities and mitma forced resettlement of populations (tens of thousands from Ecuador to Cuzco and vice versa), a practice Guaman Poma illustrates critically.

title: rebellion leader (claimant Sapa Inca)
description: Cacique of Tinta (Cuzco region), descendant of the last neo-Inca ruler Tupac Amaru I (executed 1572). Launched the largest indigenous rebellion of colonial Latin America (November 1780) against Bourbon reforms, forced labor in mines, and abusive corregidores. Captured and executed the corregidor Antonio de Arriaga (10 November 1780). Defeated at Checacupe (April 1781); captured, tortured, and on 18 May 1781 in Cuzco's Plaza de Armas forced to watch his wife Micaela Bastidas, son, uncles, and allies executed before being quartered by four horses. When this failed he was beheaded and his limbs dispatched across the Andes for display.
notable_events: 10 November 1780 execution of Arriaga, start of rebellion

sources.citation: Colección Documental del Bicentenario de la Revolución Emancipadora de Túpac Amaru, Lima, 1981 (84 vols. of primary documents).
Report of the trial and execution, Cuzco 1781, Archivo General de Indias.
Flores Galindo, Alberto. Buscando un Inca: Identidad y utopía en los Andes. Horizonte, 1988.
Walker, Charles F. The Tupac Amaru Rebellion. Belknap/Harvard, 2014.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Tupac Amaru II's execution on 18 May 1781 — preceded by the public execution of Micaela Bastidas (strangled with a garrote that could not fit her small neck, then beaten and cut in the throat), of his nine-year-old son Hipolito (forced to watch family executions before his own hanging), of his uncles — is one of the most atrocious displays of colonial violence in the Americas. Over the following decades the Bourbon crown banned Quechua, indigenous clothing, Inca imagery, and Garcilaso's Comentarios Reales. The rebellion killed ~100,000 indigenous people and 10,000-40,000 Spanish/criollo combatants and civilians (estimates in Walker 2014). Túpac Amaru II is a founding icon of Latin American anti-colonial consciousness.

title: governor-general
description: Born enslaved on the Breda plantation near Cap-Français, Saint-Domingue. Freed by 1776. Joined the 1791 slave uprising led by Boukman, emerging as the insurgents' most effective commander. Allied successively with Spain, then Revolutionary France (after the 1794 Convention abolition of slavery), negotiating a peace with the French Republic that made him Governor-General of Saint-Domingue by 1801. His 1801 constitution proclaimed him governor for life with near-absolute powers while formally keeping the colony French. Kidnapped in June 1802 by French forces under Leclerc and deported to the Fort de Joux in the French Alps, where he died of cold and neglect on 7 April 1803.
notable_events: 1794 joins French Republic after Convention abolition
7 April 1803 death at Fort de Joux

sources.citation: Toussaint Louverture, correspondence, 1790s-1803 (Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer).
James, C. L. R. The Black Jacobins: Toussaint L'Ouverture and the San Domingo Revolution. Secker & Warburg, 1938.
Dubois, Laurent. Avengers of the New World: The Story of the Haitian Revolution. Belknap/Harvard, 2004.
Popkin, Jeremy D. You Are All Free: The Haitian Revolution and the Abolition of Slavery. Cambridge, 2010.

name_variants.name: Toussaint Bréda
name_variants.context: birth name from the Bréda plantation

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Toussaint's 1801 constitution re-imposed plantation labor (cultivateurs tied to estates), abolishing formal slavery but maintaining coerced sugar/coffee production; his suppression of the 1801 Moyse rebellion (Moyse was his nephew who opposed the plantation system and forced labor) executed hundreds. This ambivalence — abolition's icon who also restored coerced labor — is documented by Dubois (2004). Napoleon's 1802 re-imposition of slavery and the Leclerc/Rochambeau expedition used mass drownings at Cap-Français and dog-hunts against black rebels, killing ~150,000 Haitians (est. Popkin 2010). Dessalines completed the revolution with independence 1804 and the 1804 massacre of French planters.

title: president/dictator
description: Caracas-born creole revolutionary who led the independence of six countries: Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Panama, and Bolivia. President of Gran Colombia (1819-1830), Peru (1824-1826), and Bolivia (1825). Delivered the Discurso de Angostura (1819) and the Carta de Jamaica (1815). Defeated the Royalist army at Boyacá (1819), Carabobo (1821), Pichincha (1822), and — with Sucre — Ayacucho (1824). Died at San Pedro Alejandrino near Santa Marta on 17 December 1830, disillusioned with Gran Colombia's dissolution.
notable_events: 1819 Battle of Boyacá; foundation of Gran Colombia
1824 Battle of Ayacucho, independence of Peru

sources.citation: Simón Bolívar. Obras Completas, 3 vols., Havana 1947.
Daniel Florencio O'Leary. Memorias del General O'Leary, 32 vols., 1879-1914.
Lynch, John. Simón Bolívar: A Life. Yale University Press, 2006.
Arana, Marie. Bolivar: American Liberator. Simon & Schuster, 2013.

name_variants.name: Simón José Antonio de la Santísima Trinidad Bolívar y Palacios Ponte-Andrade y Blanco

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Bolivar's emancipation of slaves (from 1816, progressively across republics) was militarily motivated (needed Black soldiers) and partial — slavery was fully abolished in most Gran Colombian territories only in 1852 (Venezuela 1854). His 1828 dictatorial decree and persecution of Santander's faction; the 1826 Bolivian constitution created a life-presidency that resembled monarchy. The execution of llanero general José Tomás Boves (Royalist) and Bolivar's own 1813 Decreto de Guerra a Muerte (declaring extermination of Spaniards and Canarians regardless of civilian status) set a precedent for extreme violence on both sides — estimated total deaths in the Spanish American wars of independence: 400,000-600,000. Indigenous and Afro-descendant populations fought on all sides and gained least from independence.

title: war leader
description: Seminole war leader (not a chief — Seminole polity was decentralized). Led Seminole resistance to the 1830 Indian Removal Act's application to Florida in the Second Seminole War (1835-1842). Killed the government Indian agent Wiley Thompson at Fort King (December 1835). Captured under a flag of truce by General Thomas Jesup (October 1837) — an act denounced at the time as dishonorable by many Americans. Died in prison at Fort Moultrie, South Carolina on 30 January 1838. The army surgeon Frederick Weedon decapitated his corpse and kept the head; it was later lost.
notable_events: December 1835 killing of Thompson at Fort King
December 1835 Dade Massacre (not attributed to Osceola personally)
October 1837 treacherous capture under flag of truce

sources.citation: John T. Sprague. The Origin, Progress, and Conclusion of the Florida War, 1848.
George Catlin. Letters and Notes on the Manners, Customs, and Condition of the North American Indians, 1841 (visited Osceola in prison).
Wickman, Patricia R. Osceola's Legacy. University of Alabama Press, 1991.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Second Seminole War (1835-1842) was one of the costliest in US history in per-soldier terms; ~1,500 US troops died, and an estimated 3,000-4,000 Seminoles and allied Black Seminoles (maroons) were killed or deported along the Trail of Tears to Indian Territory (Oklahoma). The 1837 seizure of Osceola under a white flag by General Jesup is documented and was controversial even in US press of the time. Weedon's postmortem decapitation is a case study in 19th-c. scientific-racism physiognomy. Florida Seminole descendants (Miccosukee Tribe, Seminole Tribe of Florida) remained uncolonized and never signed a peace treaty — the only unconquered Indigenous nation in the US.

title: king
description: Founder of the Zulu Kingdom. Illegitimate son of Senzangakhona kaJama, chief of the small Zulu clan. Served the Mthethwa paramountcy under Dingiswayo; succeeded to the Zulu chieftaincy c. 1816 after Dingiswayo's death transformed the ~1,500-person Zulu into a regional power through military reorganization (age-set regiments, short-stabbing assegai spear, bull-horn encircling formation) absorbing or destroying neighboring chiefdoms. Assassinated on 22 September 1828 at kwaDukuza by his half-brothers Dingane and Mhlangana.
notable_events: 1818 Battle of Mhlatuze River (defeat of Ndwandwe)

sources.citation: Henry Francis Fynn. The Diary of Henry Francis Fynn, ed. Stuart and Malcolm, 1950 (eyewitness, 1824-28).
Nathaniel Isaacs. Travels and Adventures in Eastern Africa, 1836 (hostile source).
James Stuart Archive, oral histories, 5 vols., ed. Webb and Wright, 1976-2014.
Hamilton, Carolyn. Terrific Majesty: The Powers of Shaka Zulu and the Limits of Historical Invention. Harvard University Press, 1998.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-004: Shaka's violence has been heavily mythologized. The 'Mfecane' (Difaqane) theory — that his wars displaced 1-2 million across southern Africa — was 20th-c. white-settler historiography used to justify land seizure by claiming empty spaces (Julian Cobbing 1988, 'The Mfecane as Alibi' JAH 29:3). Hamilton (1998) and others have partially deconstructed the myth, arguing slave-raiding from Delagoa Bay and European settler pressure were also causes of regional disruption. Shaka's military reforms and expansion were real and violent; contemporary Fynn's diary reports mass executions (though Fynn's figures of ~7,000 killed after his mother Nandi's death in 1827 are unconfirmed). The 1986-89 SABC miniseries 'Shaka Zulu' reinforced the pathological-tyrant stereotype. Honest Atlas record must note the contested historiography.

title: askia (emperor)
description: Second ruler of the Songhai Empire's Askia dynasty. A Soninke former general of Sunni Ali Ber (the conqueror of Timbuktu), he seized the throne from Sunni Baru in 1493, initiating the Islamic-legitimist Askia line. Performed the hajj (1497-98) with a reported retinue of 500 and returned as caliph of the Muslim West (recognized by the Abbasid shadow caliph in Cairo). Rebuilt Timbuktu's scholarship (Mahmud Kati, Ahmad Baba's teachers), expanded Songhai to its maximum extent from the Atlantic to Air. Blinded by his son Askia Musa in 1528, exiled to a Niger river island, where he died in 1538.

sources.citation: Mahmud Kati (and successors). Tarikh al-Fattash, c. 1519 (with later interpolations to 1660).
Abd al-Rahman al-Sa'di. Tarikh al-Sudan, c. 1655.
Hunwick, John. Timbuktu and the Songhay Empire. Brill, 1999.
Gomez, Michael A. African Dominion. Princeton, 2018.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-007: The Tarikh al-Fattash and Tarikh al-Sudan — both court-commissioned or Islamic-scholar works — portray Askia Muhammad as the pious restorer of orthodox Islam against the 'half-hearted Muslim' Sunni Ali. The condemnation of Sunni Ali in these sources is now seen as post-hoc legitimization of the Askia usurpation (Hunwick 1999). Sunni Ali was probably an accommodationist ruler drawing on both Islamic and traditional Songhai religious authority; his casting as 'apostate' served the Askias' Islamic-universalist political theology. Askia's campaigns did take slaves, and the trans-Saharan slave trade ran through his empire.

name_regnal: Ngola a Ndongo, Ngana a Matamba
title: queen regnant
description: Queen of Ndongo (1624-1626) and Matamba (1631-1663) — two Ambundu kingdoms in modern Angola. Led 37 years of war against Portuguese colonial expansion and the Atlantic slave trade. Famously at her 1622 diplomatic meeting with Portuguese governor João Correia de Sousa, ordered a servant to kneel as a chair when the Portuguese offered her a floor cushion and themselves chairs — refusing the symbolic subordination. Converted to Catholicism (baptized 'Ana de Sousa') for diplomatic leverage; later apostatized. Allied successively with the Dutch at Luanda (1641-1648), then the Imbangala mercenaries.
notable_events: 1624 succession in Ndongo, defeat by Portuguese

sources.citation: Giovanni Antonio Cavazzi da Montecuccolo. Istorica descrizione de' tre' regni Congo, Matamba et Angola, 1687 (Italian Capuchin missionary, knew her personally).
Antonio de Oliveira de Cadornega. História Geral das Guerras Angolanas, 1680-1681.
Heywood, Linda M. Njinga of Angola: Africa's Warrior Queen. Harvard University Press, 2017.
Thornton, John K. 'Legitimacy and Political Power: Queen Njinga, 1624-1663,' Journal of African History 32, 1991.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002/004: Nzinga is one of the most complex figures in anti-colonial historiography. Her diplomatic alliance with Imbangala bands (kilombo warrior societies) involved adopting or tolerating their ritualized practices including reports of infanticide within kilombo (these reports mostly from hostile Portuguese and Capuchin sources — Heywood 2017 treats with source-criticism). She did at times capture and sell slaves to Portuguese and Dutch traders, participating in the very system she resisted. Her legacy in 20th-c. Angolan nationalism (MPLA, Neto's speeches) emphasized the anti-colonial war; post-independence statues in Luanda. Her contradictory legacy as both defender of sovereignty and participant in the slave economy exemplifies the ethical complexity of African elites during the Atlantic slave era.

title: caliph
description: Fulani scholar and Sufi shaykh who led the Fulani Jihad (1804-1808), overthrowing the Hausa states of Gobir, Kano, Zazzau, Katsina, and others, and establishing the Sokoto Caliphate — one of West Africa's largest pre-colonial polities (~500,000 km²). Trained in the Qadiriyya tradition; proclaimed the hijra (1804) and then jihad against Yunfa, sarki of Gobir. His daughter Nana Asma'u was a major scholar and poet who codified Islamic education for women in the caliphate.
notable_events: 1808 conquest of Gobir, death of Yunfa
1812 abdication of temporal power to son Muhammad Bello and brother Abdullahi

sources.citation: Usman dan Fodio. Bayan Wujub al-Hijra, c. 1806.
Muhammad Bello. Infaq al-Maysur, c. 1817.
Last, Murray. The Sokoto Caliphate. Humanities Press, 1967.
Hiskett, Mervyn. The Sword of Truth: The Life and Times of Shehu Usuman dan Fodio. Oxford University Press, 1973.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Sokoto Caliphate expanded dramatically through jihad that enslaved Hausa, Yoruba, and minority populations. By the mid-19th c. Sokoto was one of the largest slaveholding societies in the world — 1-2.5 million enslaved people within its territory (Lovejoy 1981, Transformations in Slavery). Enslaved people worked rural estates (rinji) producing cotton and grain; the caliphate's wealth was substantially slave-derived. Usman dan Fodio himself wrote critically about improper enslavement of already-Muslim Hausa but the practice continued under his successors. The caliphate was destroyed by British conquest (1903 defeat of Caliph Attahiru) but its slave-holding estates survived in attenuated form until the 1930s-40s.

title: king
description: Founder and first king of a unified Hawaiian kingdom. Chief of Hawaiʻi Island from 1782. Using European firearms and the Western-built cannon of the HMS Fair American (captured 1790), conquered Maui (1790 Battle of Kepaniwai), Oʻahu (1795 Battle of Nuʻuanu Pali), and by 1810 had secured a negotiated submission of Kauaʻi's mōʻī Kaumualiʻi. Established Kamehameha's Law of the Splintered Paddle (Kānāwai Māmalahoe, 1782) protecting non-combatants in war — considered a precursor to humanitarian law.

sources.citation: Samuel M. Kamakau. Ka Moʻolelo o Kamehameha I, 1867-71 (published in Hawaiian-language newspapers).
David Malo. Moʻolelo Hawaiʻi / Hawaiian Antiquities, c. 1840.
Kuykendall, Ralph S. The Hawaiian Kingdom, vol. 1. University of Hawaiʻi, 1938.
Kameʻeleihiwa, Lilikalā. Native Land and Foreign Desires: Ko Hawaiʻi ʻĀina a me Nā Koi Puʻumake a Ka Poʻe Haole. Bishop Museum, 1992.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002/004: Kamehameha's unification involved hundreds of conquered chiefs' deaths and the post-battle human sacrifice of defeated leaders (including Keōua Kūahuʻula at Puʻukoholā Heiau in 1791). His reliance on British arms dealers (John Young and Isaac Davis became his advisors), combined with post-contact epidemics (1804 ʻōkuʻu may have killed half the Hawaiian population of Oʻahu), set in motion the demographic collapse that would reduce the Native Hawaiian population from an estimated 400,000-800,000 (1778) to 40,000 (1890). Kamehameha's successors faced this collapse, not him personally; but the unification enabled the later 1893 US-backed overthrow of his great-great-granddaughter Liliʻuokalani.

title: chief (ariki)
description: Ariki (paramount chief) of Ngāti Toa. During the 1820s-1840s musket wars, led Ngāti Toa's migration from Kāwhia southward to Kapiti Island off the southern North Island and across the Cook Strait into the northern South Island — establishing dominance over Wellington region and the northern South Island. Composed the haka 'Ka Mate' (c. 1820) after escaping enemies by hiding in a kumara pit — now famous worldwide via the All Blacks rugby team. Signed Te Tiriti o Waitangi (1840). Arrested by Governor George Grey in 1846 on charges that were never brought to trial; held for 18 months aboard HMS Calliope.
notable_events: 1830 Elizabeth affair — seizure of Ngāi Tahu chief Tamaiharanui

sources.citation: William Travers. Life and Times of Te Rauparaha, 1872.
Peter Butler (ed.). Life and Times of Te Rauparaha: by his son Tamihana Te Rauparaha, 1980 (based on 1866 Maori manuscript).
Oliver, Steven. 'Te Rauparaha,' Dictionary of New Zealand Biography, 1990.
Burns, Patricia. Te Rauparaha: A New Perspective. Reed, 1980.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Musket Wars (c. 1818-1840s) killed an estimated 20,000-40,000 Māori — roughly 20-40% of the pre-contact Māori population (King 2003). Te Rauparaha's campaigns contributed substantially, particularly against Ngāi Tahu in the 1830-33 invasions of the South Island that included the 1830 Elizabeth affair (using Captain Stewart's brig to transport Ngāti Toa warriors to attack Akaroa and capture Tamaiharanui). These campaigns involved cannibalism and the killing of non-combatants documented in both Māori and European sources. European-introduced muskets were the arms race that drove this violence; Te Rauparaha's military leadership was also a response to European settler pressure. The ambivalence — oppressor of other Māori, resister against colonial dispossession — is typical of the period.

title: queen regnant
description: Queen of Tahiti 1827-1877. Succeeded her brother Pōmare III at age 14. Her 50-year reign ran through the establishment of the French protectorate (1842, over her protest), her exile to Raiatea (1844-46), the Franco-Tahitian war (1844-47), and the progressive destruction of Tahitian sovereignty. The 1842 coercion by Admiral Dupetit-Thouars imposed French protection at gunpoint; her appeals to Queen Victoria were declined. Under her reign Tahiti was converted to Protestantism (she was baptized 1819) and subjected to a colonial legal system.

sources.citation: Archives de la Polynésie française, Papeete, correspondance 1827-1877.
George Pritchard. The Aggressions of the French at Tahiti, 1866.
Matsuda, Matt K. Empire of Love: Histories of France and the Pacific. Oxford University Press, 2005.
Newbury, Colin. Tahiti Nui: Change and Survival in French Polynesia 1767-1945. University of Hawaii Press, 1980.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Pōmare's reign is a case study in colonial coercion dressed as protectorate. French naval superiority forced acceptance of 'protection' she did not want. Her son Pōmare V (1877-1880) was pressured into abdicating all sovereignty in 1880, full annexation. Tahitian population had collapsed from ~40,000 (1767 Cook) to ~6,000 (1865) from introduced diseases. The missionary and French colonial record systematically disparaged Tahitian women's status (they had held substantial political power — all four Pōmare rulers ruled through female chiefly descent) to justify the 'civilizing mission'.

title: huey tlahtoani
description: Eleventh and last huey tlahtoani of Tenochtitlan. Succeeded Cuitláhuac (who died of smallpox in 1520) at age ~25. Led the Mexica defense during the 75-day siege of Tenochtitlan by Cortés (May-August 1521). Captured on 13 August 1521 while attempting to escape across Lake Texcoco. Tortured (feet burned) by the Spanish seeking the hidden Mexica treasure. Hanged in 1525 in Chiapas on charges of plotting revolt. His cadaver was discovered in 1949 under a chapel in Ixcateopan, Guerrero — though authenticity of the remains is contested.

sources.citation: Hernán Cortés. Cartas de Relación (Cartas Segunda, Tercera), 1520-22.
Bernardino de Sahagun. Florentine Codex, book 12.
Hassig, Ross. Mexico and the Spanish Conquest. Longman, 1994.
Townsend, Camilla. Fifth Sun: A New History of the Aztecs. Oxford University Press, 2019.

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: Cuauhtémoc's torture by Pedro de Alvarado in 1521 (feet held over fire to reveal the location of the 'Moctezuma treasure' stolen from Cortés on the Noche Triste) is documented in Cortes's own third letter and Sahagun. His execution in 1525 in Chiapas on charges of conspiring revolt during Cortés's Honduran expedition — the evidence was a dream account reported by Mexicaltzingo lord Mexicalcingo — is widely considered by modern scholars a judicial murder to eliminate residual legitimate authority. The Mexica population collapsed from ~1.5 million (1519, Valley of Mexico) to ~300,000 (1568) through smallpox (1520), cocoliztli (1545, 1576 — now identified as probable Salmonella enterica), and forced labor in the encomienda. Cortés should be described as a conquistador responsible for one of history's major genocides.
